learning_objective: Can use 'this' in an increasing range of contexts to refer to places and things from the speaker's or writer's point of view. ► noun phrases ► pronouns: demonstrative

learning_objective: Can use the possessive pronoun 'mine', with singular and plural reference, in subject and object positions, and complement positions after 'be' and after prepositions.
example_sentence: I bought a new jacket because mine was very old.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of pronouns ('all', 'both') with 'of' followed by an object pronoun, to refer to quantity.
example_sentence: There are a lot of functions but I don't know how to use all of them.

learning_objective: Can use pronouns 'some' and 'any', to refer to quantity.
example_sentence: [talking about items of clothing] Some were cheap but some were expensive.

learning_objective: Can use 'some' as a subject and object to refer back to a plural or uncountable noun.
example_sentence: I saw a lot of people on the beach, some were playing football and some were sunbathing. (A2 WAYSTAGE; 2011; Somali; Pass

learning_objective: Can use 'any' as an object in a negative clause to refer back to a plural or uncountable noun.
example_sentence: Bring some good CDs. I don't have any.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of singular reflexive pronouns ('myself', 'yourself') with a limited range of verbs ('enjoy', 'buy') to refer to actions where the subject and object of the verb are the same.
example_sentence: I enjoyed myself so much.

learning_objective: Can leave out the subject pronoun with 'hope'. ► ellipsis
example_sentence: Hope to see you soon.

learning_objective: Can use 'it' as an empty subject when there is nothing else to put in subject position, particularly with weather or time.
example_sentence: It's warm and windy.

learning_objective: Can use 'one' as a pronoun to substitute for singular countable nouns which have already been mentioned or are obvious from the context.
example_sentence: [talking about a phone] I chose the black one with the numbers in orange.

learning_objective: Can use 'the one' and 'the' + pre-modifier + 'one' with a complement, to refer to something specific.
example_sentence: My house is No.8 and it's the one with silver gates.

learning_objective: Can use determiners + premodifers + 'one'.
example_sentence: I like my present very much because I needed a new one.

learning_objective: Can form alternative questions with two words from the same class combined with 'or'.
example_sentence: Can you bring some food or drinks for the dinner?

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of question tags.
example_sentence: You don't know where I live, do you?

learning_objective: Can use 'wh-'words + auxiliary 'have' to form 'wh-'questions. ► present perfect  ►  past perfect
example_sentence: How have you been?

learning_objective: Can use 'wh-'words + auxiliary 'do' + subject + main verb to form 'wh-'questions. ► present simple;  ► past simple
example_sentence: What do you think?

learning_objective: Can use 'wh-'words + auxiliary 'be' to form 'wh-'questions. ► present continuous ► past continuous
example_sentence: Who are you going with?

learning_objective: Can use 'wh-'words + modal verbs + subject + main verb to form 'wh-'questions.
example_sentence: What will you bring?

learning_objective: Can use negative question 'why don’t' + pronoun + verb, to make a suggestion or invitation.
example_sentence: Why don't we watch a film?

learning_objective: Can use 'wh-'words + main verb 'be' + subject to form 'wh-' questions.
example_sentence: How was your dinner yesterday?

learning_objective: Can use auxiliary 'be' + subject + the '-ing' form (continuous form) to form 'yes/no' questions.
example_sentence: Are you going to come?

learning_objective: Can use auxiliary 'do' + subject + main verb to form 'yes/no' questions.
example_sentence: My favourite song is 'Viva la vida'. Do you know it?

learning_objective: Can use auxiliary 'have' + subject + the '-ed' form to form 'yes/no' questions.  ►  present perfect
example_sentence: I've just watched a football competition with my family and friends. Have you watched it?

learning_objective: Can use main verb 'be' + subject to form 'yes/no' questions.
example_sentence: Are you ok?

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of modal verbs + subject + main verb to form 'yes/no' questions.
example_sentence: Could you meet me at my home at 7 pm?

guideword: FORM: REPORTED STATEMENTS WITH 'TELL', PRONOUN SHIFT
learning_objective: Can report statements using a reporting clause with 'tell' + direct object + 'that-'clause, with a pronoun shift where relevant.
example_sentence: [about a mobile phone] It's very important to me to have it with me because my brother is in the hospital and my father told me that he will call.

guideword: FORM: REPORTED STATEMENTS WITH 'SAY', PRONOUN SHIFT
learning_objective: Can report statements using a reporting clause with 'say' + 'that-'clause, with a pronoun shift where relevant.
example_sentence: My mother said that she can take us there.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of linking verbs with complements.
example_sentence: It looks good on you.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of verbs, typically reporting, with a 'that'-clause as the direct object.
example_sentence: I hope that you'll come.

learning_objective: Can use verbs, typically reporting verbs, followed by a noun or pronoun as the indirect object and a clause with or without 'that', as the direct object.
example_sentence: I told you that she has a new car?

guideword: FORM: VERBS + DIRECT OBJECT CLAUSE WITHOUT 'THAT'
learning_objective: Can use reporting verbs, especially mental process verbs, with a clause as the direct object, without 'that', especially in informal contexts.
example_sentence: I hope you can go, buddy.

learning_objective: Can use verbs expressing preference followed either by a 'to'-infinitive or an '-ing' form, with no change in meaning.
example_sentence: I love to play football.

learning_objective: Can use 'enjoy' followed by the '-ing' form.
example_sentence: We enjoy playing the piano and singing together.

learning_objective: Can use 'would love to' followed by the 'to'-infinitive to give emphasis.
example_sentence: I'd love to do the concert.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of verbs followed by a 'to'-infinitive.
example_sentence: I bought them because I hope to go to England this year and the weather isn't very good.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of phrasal verbs without an object.
example_sentence: I'm going to get up early on Saturday.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of phrasal verbs + object pronoun + particle.
example_sentence: I will pick you up and arrange everything.

learning_objective: Can use 'look forward to' as a fixed expression followed by an '-ing' form or noun phrase, usually at the end of correspondence.
example_sentence: I'm looking forward to seeing you!

learning_objective: Can use 'there is' + uncountable nouns as complement.
example_sentence: I need it because there is some money in it which I have to pay to someone.

learning_objective: Can use both 'there is' and 'there are' + 'a lot of' + plural noun.
example_sentence: There's a lot of people there.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of modal auxiliary verbs.
example_sentence: And I think we should go somewhere else to have a drink after the film.

learning_objective: Can use linking verbs with adjective complements.
example_sentence: It makes me feel comfortable and the room looks bigger with the colour.

learning_objective: Can use auxiliary verbs 'have' and 'do'.
example_sentence: It has been a long time since I saw you.

learning_objective: Can use semi-modal auxiliary verb 'have (got) to'.
example_sentence: You've got to bring paintbrushes, paper and paint, that's all.

guideword: FORM: COMBINING MORE THAN TWO ADJECTIVES
learning_objective: Can use commas and 'and' to join more than two adjectives, after 'be'.
example_sentence: Her name is Cassia, she is beautiful, intelligent, funny and very reliable.

guideword: FORM: COMBINING THE SAME COMPARATIVE ADJECTIVE WITH 'AND'
learning_objective: Can use 'and' to repeat a comparative adjective to indicate change over time, usually after 'become' or get. ► adjectives: comparatives
example_sentence: I could see how the fire was burning the door, and the room became hotter and hotter.

learning_objective: Can use a comma to combine two adjectives used before the noun, following the usual order of adjective types.
example_sentence: It was the biggest TV in the bazaar, with its huge, black screen.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of compound adjectives ('good-looking', 'well-known')
example_sentence: He was tall and good-looking.

learning_objective: Can use 'and' to join a limited range of comparative adjectives.► adjectives: comparatives
example_sentence: In the winter you can go to the fitness centre, there you can get stronger and fitter.

learning_objective: Can use 'and' to repeat a comparative adjective to indicate change over time, usually after 'become' or 'get'.  ► adjectives: comparatives
example_sentence: I saw everything become smaller and smaller.

learning_objective: Can use '(so) much' to modify and intensify comparative adjectives used predicatively after a verb, usually 'be'.
example_sentence: I think the teacher in the new class is much better than the last one.

learning_objective: Can use 'a (little) bit' to modify comparative adjectives used predicatively after a verb, usually 'be'.
example_sentence: He's 15, a bit smaller than me, dark hair, brown eyes.

learning_objective: Can use 'even' to modify and intensify comparative adjectives used predicatively after a verb, usually 'be' and 'get'.
example_sentence: [talking about a series of films] I'm sure you've seen part one already, but part two was even better!

learning_objective: Can use 'too' before adjectives followed by 'to'-infinitive.
example_sentence: He always told me that he was too busy to write a letter to me, his wife.

learning_objective: Can use 'enough' to modify adjectives.
example_sentence: My room is big enough, there is a lot of space, and for this reason I will buy a stereo.

learning_objective: Can use adjective phrases attributively, before a noun.
example_sentence: … my boss told me that at 10.15 am I have a very important meeting.

learning_objective: Can form adjective phrases with 'quite a' + adjective.
example_sentence: I been thinking about your last letter for quite a long time and I came to the following conclusion.

learning_objective: Can use adjectives with the prefix a- ('asleep', 'awake', 'alive', 'alone') predicatively only, after linking verbs.
example_sentence: I was very nervious because I was  alone at the bus station …

learning_objective:
Can use an increasing range of adjectives attributively, before a noun.
► noun phrases
example_sentence: The main character in the programme is a journalist who is visiting the town.

learning_objective: Can use adjectives as object complement after 'make'.
example_sentence: That's something that makes me angry!

learning_objective: Can use an adjective or adjective phrase after pronouns like 'something', 'nothing', 'somewhere', 'nowhere'.
example_sentence: I like cooking and I want to cook something special.

learning_objective: Can form an increasing range of complex noun phrases with a superlative adjective + prepositional phrase, to talk about something unique. ► noun phrases
example_sentence: Yesterday was the most exciting day of my life!

guideword: FORM/USE: 'THE BEST' WITH NOUN AND PRESENT PERFECT
learning_objective: Can use 'the best' before a noun + present perfect to talk about a unique experience.
example_sentence: It was the best birthday present I have ever had.

guideword: FORM: 'THE BEST' WITH NOUN AND  'TO-' INFINITIVE
learning_objective: Can use 'the best' before a noun + 'to'-infinitive.  ► clauses: comparative
example_sentence: Summer is the best time to visit Poland.

learning_objective: Can use 'one of the' before a superlative adjective, followed by a plural noun. ► noun phrases
example_sentence: It was one of the best days of my life.

learning_objective: Can form noun phrases with a range of determiners before a superlative adjective. ► noun phrases
example_sentence: There, people wear their best clothes.

learning_objective: Can modify an increasing range of adverbs with other adverbs.► adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
example_sentence: We get along quite well.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of adverbs to refer to the timing of an event.
example_sentence: Dear Kate, I went to the cinema with a new friend recently.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of manner adverbs and adverb phrases to modify how something happens.
example_sentence: They are like my sisters. We go everywhere together.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of adverbs and adverb phrases ('near', 'far away', 'upstairs', 'downstairs') to indicate place.
example_sentence: You can't go far away because I don't think your parents want to pay for it.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use 'really really' with verbs and adjectives for emphasis.
example_sentence: Oh, I really really like her very friendly and lovely smile.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of degree adverbs ('a little', 'a bit') with verbs to indicate degree or intensity. ► determiners
example_sentence: When I came to England, one year ago, I didn't know any English word, now I'm very happy because I don't speak very well but I can understand and speak a little.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of adverbs ('therefore', 'furthermore', 'otherwise') to show a relationship between two clauses or sentences.
example_sentence: You don't like city's with a lot of people. Therefore I suggest you go to a small school in the countryside, near your new home.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of focusing adverbs ('particularly', 'especially') to point to or highlight something. ► adverbs: position
example_sentence: I usually read in my free time but particularly in the evenings before I go to the bed.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use adverbs as discourse markers to organise text.  ►  adverbs: position ►  Discourse markers
example_sentence: Firstly, I advise you to visit both schools.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of adverbs ('completely', 'obviously', 'clearly', 'actually', 'luckily', 'honestly', 'sadly', 'basically', 'obviously', 'hopefully') to indicate an attitude or viewpoint.
example_sentence: I am completely sure.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of adverbs to indicate degrees of certainty.
example_sentence: [talking about a film] And it will probably be on TV next month!

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of manner adverbs to modify verbs.
example_sentence: You have to choose carefully.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of degree adverbs to modify adverbs. ► adverb phrases: form
example_sentence: We left really early on a Friday morning.

learning_objective: Can use adverbs to modify clauses and sentences to indicate stance or attitude.
example_sentence: But, obviously, you prefer going with your friends because you will have a better time.

learning_objective: Can use 'than' + a finite clause forming the second part of a comparison after a comparative adjective
example_sentence: I wish our schools could become better than they are. (Georgia (Republic of); B1 THRESHOLD; 2007; Georgian; Pass)

learning_objective: Can use 'than' + a limited range of non-finite clauses (with -ing), forming the second part of a comparison after a comparative adjective
example_sentence: Well, If I were you I would go to a small school in the countryside because it's less complicated than going to a large school in a town.

learning_objective: Can use 'rather than' + noun phrase or prepositional phrase to stress one element in a comparison.
example_sentence: Use the stairs rather than the lift, the bike rather than the tube and whenever you've got enough time, try running around the neighbourhood in the morning before going to work.

learning_objective: Can use '(not) as' + adjective/adverb + 'as' to introduce a clause (often with a substitute auxiliary verb) to talk about two things being equal or unequal in some way.
example_sentence: When I come back from the hospital, I will try to cover the work as much as I can.

learning_objective: Can use 'too' + adjective + 'to'-infinitive.
example_sentence: First of all, I think you are too young to go on holiday by yourself.

learning_objective: Can use 'the best (that)' followed by a clause with the present perfect to talk about something unique.
example_sentence: It's the best place that I have ever seen.

guideword: FORM: 'THE SAME' (+ NOUN) + 'AS' + PRONOUN OR NOUN
learning_objective: Can use 'the same' (+ noun) + 'as' + pronoun or noun.
example_sentence: I wasn't the only one in my class, there were many others at the same level as me.

learning_objective: Can use 'so' + adjective + 'that' clause.
example_sentence: I was so tired that I didn't have the strength to stand up and ask him for a photograph.

guideword: FORM: SENSE VERBS + 'AS IF' OR 'AS THOUGH' + FINITE CLAUSE
learning_objective: Can use verbs of the senses + 'as if' or 'as though' + finite clause.
example_sentence: I was excited and I felt as if I was a bird.

learning_objective: Can use linking verbs + 'like' or 'similar to'.
example_sentence: Some people have told me that I look like a pop star when I wear it.

guideword: FORM/USE: PRESENT SIMPLE 'IF' CLAUSE + MODAL, FUTURE, POSSIBLE OUTCOME
learning_objective: Can use 'if' + present simple to introduce a possible future condition, with modal verbs in the main clause, to talk about a possible result. ► modality
example_sentence: I think that it would be very good if you start going to the gym or you start cycling.

guideword: FORM/USE: PRESENT SIMPLE 'IF' CLAUSE + 'WILL', FUTURE, LIKELY OUTCOME (FIRST CONDITIONAL)
learning_objective: Can use 'if' + present simple to introduce a possible future condition, with 'will' in the main clause to talk about a likely result.
example_sentence: I talked to my parents and I think if you tell them your reasons, they will accept it.

guideword: FORM/USE: PRESENT CONTINUOUS 'IF' CLAUSE , PLANS & ADVICE
learning_objective: Can use 'if' + present continuous or 'going to' to introduce a possible plan, with modal verbs or imperatives in the main clause to give advice.
example_sentence: Billy, if you are moving you should think a lot, first you have to go and see the two schools.

guideword: FORM/USE: 'IF' + PAST SIMPLE + 'WOULD', FUTURE, IMAGINED SITUATION|(SECOND CONDITIONAL)
learning_objective: Can form conditional subordinate clauses with 'if' + past simple, and would in the main clause, to talk about an imagined situation, often in the context of advice or opinion-giving.
example_sentence: Maybe it would be more fun if you went with your friends.

guideword: FORM/USE: 'IF' + PAST PERFECT + 'WOULD HAVE' + '-ED', IMAGINED SITUATION, REGRET|(THIRD CONDITIONAL)
learning_objective: Can form conditional subordinate clauses with 'if' + past perfect simple and 'would have' + '-ed' in the main clause, to talk about imagined situations in the past, often with regret.
example_sentence: If they had not been there, I would have been very lonely.

guideword: FORM/USE: 'IF' + PAST SIMPLE + 'COULD', FUTURE, IMAGINED SITUATION
learning_objective: Can form conditional subordinate clauses with 'if' + past simple, and 'could' in the main clause to talk about an imagined situation, often in the context of suggesting.
example_sentence: If you wanted to do some outdoor activities, we could go fishing or even walking through the green fields.

guideword: FORM/USE: 'IF I WERE YOU' + 'WOULD', FUTURE, IMAGINED SITUATION
learning_objective: Can use 'if I were you' and 'would' in the main clause often in the context of advice or opinion-giving.
example_sentence: Well, if I were you I would go to a small school in the countryside because it's less complicated than going to a large school in a town.

learning_objective: Can use 'unless' + present simple to talk about an exception, with present and future reference.
example_sentence: I think I am going to pay by cash, unless you accept credit cards.

learning_objective: Can use 'If not' as a conditional clause to offer an alternative to refer to a previous direct or indirect 'yes-no' question where the answer might be ‘no’.
example_sentence: Is the Sheraton Hotel ok for you? If not, call me as soon as possible.

learning_objective: Can use 'If so' as a conditional clause to refer a previous direct or indirect 'yes-no' question where the answer might be ‘yes’. ► Clauses coordinated
example_sentence: Do you think we could have an extra class? If so, I'd like to do it to cover the work I'll miss.

guideword: FORM: COMBINING CLAUSES OF THE SAME TYPE
learning_objective: Can combine clauses of the same type, main or subordinate, finite or non-finite, with conjunctions.
example_sentence: You will have a great time with your friends, because I know they are going to the seaside and staying in a hotel near the sea. (Georgia (Republic of); B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; Georgian; Pass)

learning_objective: Can combine multiple main clauses with a range of conjunctions, often in a narrative context.
example_sentence: By the time his mother arrived home, the suitcase was still lost, so Larry decided to tell her the truth, but then his mother showed him his suitcase and told Larry that he left it in the car.

learning_objective: Can combine two main clauses of the same type using 'either' in front or mid position, followed by 'or' to introduce the second clause.
example_sentence: Regarding your future plans, we would like to know which city you are going to move to, so that if we get the chance, we can either visit you or write to you.

learning_objective: Can use the auxiliary verb 'do' in an affirmative declarative clause, for emphasis and affirmation.
example_sentence: Yes, I do have a favorite restaurant.

learning_objective: Can use 'do' + base form of a main verb, for emphasis or in formal contexts.
example_sentence: Do write back and let me know all your news.

learning_objective: Can use the imperative to make an offer or an invitation.
example_sentence: Come and visit us after the New Year, we've got so much to talk about.

learning_objective: Can use an imperative clause with 'let me' + base form of a main verb, as a focusing device, to introduce something
example_sentence: Let me tell you my story.

learning_objective: Can form questions with a 'wh-' word as subject, without an auxiliary verb.
example_sentence: What happened?

learning_objective: Can form interrogative clauses with an adverb in mid position, between the subject and the main verb.
example_sentence: Have you ever been to Marseille?

learning_objective: Can use 'How' + adjective.
example_sentence: How exciting!

learning_objective: Can use 'How' + adjective + clause.
example_sentence: How lucky you are to have visited that exhibition!

learning_objective: Can use defining relative clauses, 'the person who/that, the thing that, the (only) one who/that' as a focusing device.
example_sentence: The thing that was great is that the weather was really warm and it didn't rain.

learning_objective: Can use defining relative clauses with 'where' to define nouns referring to place.
example_sentence: The city where I live is very nice and extremely charming.

learning_objective: Can use defining relative clauses with 'when' to define nouns relating to time.
example_sentence: The day when he had to go back to Korea, I could not stop myself crying.

learning_objective: Can use a non-defining relative clause with 'who' as the object.
example_sentence: What I liked most was the friend I made there, a green-eyed chubby boy, who I played with till dinner time, when I had to go home.

learning_objective: Can use defining relative clauses with 'why' after 'reason', to give an explanation and for focus.
example_sentence: The reason why I love it is that it's quite original because it's about a woman who can see ghosts.

learning_objective: Can use a defining relative clause with 'who' or 'that' as the object.
example_sentence: My friends are the people who I love most.

learning_objective: Can use defining and non-defining relative clauses with 'whose name'.
example_sentence: We have a new employee whose name is Mary Watts.

learning_objective: Can use a finite subordinate clause with conjunctions ('even though, (al)though, while if') to introduce contrast, before or after a main clause.
example_sentence: You should come with your sister even though you would rather be alone.

learning_objective: Can use a finite subordinate clause with conjunctions to introduce reasons, after a main clause.
example_sentence: I prefer to speak on 14 July, as I have got another appointment on the next day.

learning_objective: Can use a finite subordinate clause with conjunctions ('so (that), in order that') to introduce purpose and result, after a main clause.
example_sentence: I think it is important to write about this topic so that young students and other people who are working with business, can get some inspiration.

learning_objective: Can use a non-finite subordinate clause with 'before' and 'after' + '-ing', before or after a main clause, to refer to time.
example_sentence: Instead I buy books once a month and I read them in the evening before going to bed.

learning_objective: Can use 'either … or' to connect two words, phrases or clauses.
example_sentence: The men wear suits, either white or black, and the bride and women wear special long dresses, usually the same colour.

learning_objective: Can use 'plus' to connect clauses and sentences, often to point out a positive addition or advantage.
example_sentence: I would be more than pleased to receive you at home, it's not too big, but we could have a lot of fun, plus you won't have to pay a thing!

learning_objective: Can use a range of conjunctions ('and, but, or, so, then') to combine more complex strings of clauses and sentences.
example_sentence: I was scared and closed the window, but I couldn't sleep, so a few minutes later I opened the window again, but they were gone.

learning_objective: Can use 'plus' as coordinating conjunction, often in relation to numbers.
example_sentence: In my house we are six people, my mother, my father, my sister and my two brothers, plus my two cats, which are members of the family too.

learning_objective: Can use 'both … and' to combine noun phrases, to give emphasis.
example_sentence: I felt sorry for you because both friends and family are very important and having to pick one is very hard.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of simple subordinating conjunctions ('as, after, before since, until, although, whether, so (that), though') to introduce a subordinate clause.
example_sentence: In fact, I have to buy a desk, as I don't have one.

learning_objective: Can use no article before an increasing range of nouns in some fixed expressions with prepositions.
example_sentence: The weather is very cold at night.

learning_objective: Can use 'another' to talk about something additional.
example_sentence: Dear Mary, I spent a week in Dubai and I am going to spend another week in Al Ain.

learning_objective: Can use no article before an increasing range of singular and plural nouns when referring to things in general.
example_sentence: Education in the future will change in a good way.

learning_objective: Can use 'another' with singular nouns. ► pronouns ► determiners
example_sentence: Every 5 minutes, he makes another great joke.

learning_objective: Can use 'another' to talk about something different.
example_sentence: I'm sorry but I won't be able to go to the English class tomorrow because I'll go to Maldonado to see my mother that is ill. But I can go another day to cover the work I've missed.

learning_objective: Can use 'the other' with a singular noun to refer to the second or the opposite of two things, and with a plural noun to refer to one of a set of things. ► pronouns ►  noun phrases
example_sentence: She saw his blue jacket in a shop on the other side of the street.

learning_objective: Can use 'this' with time and date words to refer to the past.
example_sentence: Hi Mark, Thank you for your lovely letter which I received this morning.

learning_objective: Can use ''s' after irregular plural nouns to indicate possession.
example_sentence: When I go out with my friends, I actually like wearing clothes that attract people's attention.

learning_objective: Can use possessive determiner 'their'. ► noun phrases ► possessive pronouns
example_sentence: They were having the time of their lives, watching all the animals the zoo had, but when they returned to their car, they saw that someone was stealing Paul's suitcase.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of quantifying determiners + 'of' + determiner ('half of', 'enough of', 'none of',).► pronouns
example_sentence: In my opinion you should spend half of your holiday with your parents and the other half with your friends: I think this would be the best thing to do.

guideword: FORM: WITH PLURAL AND UNCOUNTABLE NOUNS
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of quantifying determiners with both plural nouns and uncountable nouns ('most', 'enough', 'plenty of', 'loads of').
example_sentence: I'm convinced that most people watch too much TV these days.

guideword: FORM: 'SO MUCH', 'TOO MUCH' WITH UNCOUNTABLE NOUNS
learning_objective: Can use 'so much' and 'too much' with uncountable nouns.
example_sentence: Hello Marina. Unfortunately I have so much work at the moment that I can't help you.

learning_objective: Can use 'much' with uncountable nouns in interrogative contexts.
Can use quantifying determiners with uncountable nouns, often in informal and/or spoken contexts ('a little', 'a bit of' and 'a little bit of').
example_sentence: I went with my friends so we could spend a little time together.

learning_objective: Can use 'many' with plural nouns in interrogative contexts.
example_sentence: Have you got many friends in England?

learning_objective: Can use a wide range of quantifying determiners with plural nouns ('several', 'millions of', 'a few of').
example_sentence: So several days ago we received a complaint.

learning_objective: Can modify determiners with adverbs. ► adverbs
example_sentence: Because almost all of the programmes are stupid.

learning_objective: Can use a range of words and phrases as discourse markers to add information.
example_sentence: Moreover the health of our staff and customers is essential.

guideword: FORM/USE: ORGANISING, MARKING NEW KNOWLEDGE, INFORMAL
learning_objective: Can use 'you see', 'the thing is' as discourse markers to mark new knowledge, usually in informal contexts.
example_sentence: You see, in the park, you can enjoy fresh air…

learning_objective: Can use sequencing adverbs as discourse markers to organise text.
example_sentence: Firstly, we need a good expert to run the course. Secondly, good materials are needed.

learning_objective: Can use a range of phrases as discourse markers to introduce contrasting statements.
example_sentence: On the one hand going to a large school is better for making friends, but on the other hand I think you can learn better in a small school.

learning_objective: Can use adverbs as discourse markers to indicate an attitude or viewpoint.
example_sentence: Unfortunately, they filmed only the building and the garden, not the students!

learning_objective: Can use adverbs in the front position for focus.
example_sentence: That's my diary, the dearest thing I had. Here I wrote how I felt, what I did, who I met and all my problems.

learning_objective: Can use 'it' + 'be' + adjective + 'that-'clause for focus.
example_sentence: It's great that you have got a new job!

learning_objective: Can use the affirmative form with 'will' and 'shall'.
example_sentence: However, I shall be attending class the day after tomorrow and I shall cover the work I miss by copying it from John.

learning_objective: Can use the negative form with 'will' ('won’t').
example_sentence: I would like you to know that I won't be attending your English class next week.

learning_objective: Can use the simple past form of 'be' + 'going to' to talk about the future from a point in the past. ► 'be going to'
example_sentence: She was going to return it but she saw something very strange, her number was on the screen.

learning_objective: Can use 'would' to talk about the future from a point in the past.► 'would'
example_sentence: I've just bought a new dress, and a beautiful black T-shirt. When I saw them, I thought they would be great for tonight's party.

learning_objective: Can use 'shall' with 'I' and 'we' to talk about plans and intentions.
example_sentence: I promise that I shall cover all the work and give it to you tomorrow.

learning_objective: Can use 'will' and '‘ll' to make predictions.
example_sentence: It will be hotter because of global warming.

learning_objective: Can use 'will' to talk about fixed plans, often with timetabled times and dates.
example_sentence: The work will start at 8 a.m. and finish at 2 p.m. The new schedule starts on 1 June.

learning_objective: Can use the negative form.
example_sentence: I am not going to go to school tomorrow.

learning_objective: Can use the past form of 'be' + 'going to'. ► future in the past
example_sentence: When we realized it was going to be an extremely hot day, we took water.

learning_objective: Can use 'be' + 'going to' with a limited range of adverbs, after the auxiliary be form, in the normal mid position.
example_sentence: I know that I 'm never going to forget this moment.

learning_objective: Can use the negative form of 'be going to' to talk about plans and intentions.
example_sentence: I 'm not going to be able to meet you next Tuesday, because I have to go to the dentist at 5.30.

learning_objective: Can use the past form of 'be going to' to refer to the future from a point in the past. ► future in the past
example_sentence: She didn't know what was going to happen.

learning_objective: Can use the past form of 'be going to' as the reported form.► reported speech
example_sentence: Fortunately, he was told his boss was going to be a bit late, so he had some time to go back home.

learning_objective: Can use 'be going to' with an increasing range of verbs to make predictions.
example_sentence: I think you're going to like it.

learning_objective: Can use the past form of 'be going to' (often followed by 'but') to talk about a plan, sometimes one that may have changed.
example_sentence: We were going to get married two months ago but we broke up and I really didn't know why.

learning_objective: Can use the present continuous with an increasing range of verbs to talk about future arrangements.
example_sentence: I’m attending my grandmother’s funeral tomorrow.

learning_objective: Can use the present continuous with an increasing range of verbs to ask about future plans.
example_sentence: Are you visiting my country on your next holiday?

learning_objective: Can use the present simple with 'as soon as' to refer to the future.
example_sentence: I'm planning to move as soon as I finish college because job opportunities in my area aren't good.

learning_objective: Can use 'it' + 'be' + 'important' + ('that') clause.
example_sentence: It's important that you take breaks and don't work all the time.

learning_objective: Can use 'if-' clauses + 'possible', as a politeness strategy.
example_sentence: If it is possible I would like to pay by credit card.

learning_objective: Can use 'it' + 'be' + adjective + 'to' + infinitive, to make an assertion more or less direct.
example_sentence: Our sales department staff are heavily involved in several international projects, so it is necessary to improve their foreign language skills.

learning_objective: Can use 'it' + 'be' + adjective + 'for' + object + 'to' + infinitive.
example_sentence: If you have to choose between two schools, I think it's important for you to know what they’re like.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of adverbs, for emphasis.
example_sentence: If I were you I'd certainly choose not to go with your parents.

guideword: FORM/USE: MODAL VERB + MODAL ADVERB, HEDGING OR EMPHASIS
learning_objective: Can use modal verb + modal adverb to modify an assertion, either through hedging or emphasis.
example_sentence: Next year you can surely go somewhere with your friends.

learning_objective: Can use 'could' + subject + 'possibly' to make requests more polite.
example_sentence: Could I possibly see you at 3.30?

learning_objective: Can use the negative question forms in main clauses and question tags.
example_sentence: Can't you go with your parents some time and with your friends later?

learning_objective: Can use 'can' with a limited range of adverbs (including 'also', 'always', 'even', 'just', 'only', 'really', 'still') in the normal mid position after the modal verb.   ► adverbs
example_sentence: You can also write postcards and letters to your friends.

learning_objective: Can use 'can be' to talk about general truths and tendencies.
example_sentence: … spending an entire summer without your friends can be very boring.

learning_objective: Can use 'can you believe' to express surprise.
example_sentence: They invented a robot which plays football, can you believe this?

learning_objective: Can use the affirmative form of 'could' to talk about ability.
example_sentence: … she could hear a man talking, he was giving her orders…

learning_objective: Can use 'could I' to seek permission.
example_sentence: I am in the basketball team of my school and tomorrow there is an important game. Could I take the class that I will miss on Saturday?

learning_objective: Can use 'could' to talk about possibility.
example_sentence: But believe me, it could be really fun to have a talkative person as your friend, he or she will give you good laugh at anytime!

learning_objective: Can use 'could' with an increasing range of verbs to make suggestions.
example_sentence: …you could become a member of a gym.

learning_objective: Can use a range of modal verbs + 'be able to' + infinitive.
example_sentence: After finishing school, you might be able to hang out with your friends.

learning_objective: Can use 'be allowed to' to talk about permission.
example_sentence: My mother went with me on this trip because I wasn't allowed to go by myself.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of expressions with 'be' + infinitive ('be allowed to', 'be supposed to', 'be able to') with present and past forms of 'be' and with modal 'will'.
example_sentence: First of all, if you are allowed to go out of the building in your break, you should do it.

learning_objective: Can use 'was supposed to', 'were supposed to' to refer to past or future arrangements which may not have happened or may change.
example_sentence: I am really sorry, but I will have to make some changes to my trip. I was supposed to arrive on 11th June 2003, but I will arrive the next month.

learning_objective: Can use 'was able to', 'were able to' to talk about facts in the past.
example_sentence: [about a bus journey] She was able to get a good seat and she unexpectedly got to school early.

learning_objective: Can use the fixed expression 'I have to admit' for emphasis.
example_sentence: I have to admit that I watch a lot of TV, specially movies and soap operas, I love them.

learning_objective: Can use 'have to' as an infinitive form.
example_sentence: I am going to have to buy new curtains.

learning_objective: Can use 'may' with a limited range of adverbs (most commonly 'also') in the normal mid-position after the modal verb. ► adverbs
example_sentence: I may also ask you for another favour....

learning_objective: Can use the negative form.
example_sentence: I knew I may not meet him again.

learning_objective: Can use the question form with 'I'.
example_sentence: May I give you a short presentation on 17th December?

learning_objective: Can use 'may I' to ask for permission.
example_sentence: May I be your guide while you stay here?

learning_objective: Can use 'might' to make polite suggestions and give advice.
example_sentence: The park is a great place for picnic. It has lots of trees, a beautiful view and fresh air. You might need to bring some food and drink.

learning_objective: Can use 'might have' + '-ed' to talk about possibility with a past reference.
example_sentence: Many things might have led to these changes.

learning_objective: Can use 'might have' + '-ed'.
example_sentence: [talking about an unusual sound] It might have been our neighbours' cat.

learning_objective: Can use 'might' as the past form of 'may' in indirect reports.
example_sentence: … my parents said it might be the last holiday where we are all together.

learning_objective: Can use the negative form.
example_sentence: I like to wear a shirt and pants. It might not be very fashionable, it might look simple, but that's the way I like it.

learning_objective: Can use 'might' in phrases, such as 'as you might know', 'have already heard', to focus the reader on shared knowledge.
example_sentence: As you might know, nobody came here until Wednesday afternoon.

learning_objective: Can use 'must' to make invitations.
example_sentence: You must come and stay in our house too.

learning_objective: Can use 'must' with a limited range of adverbs (most commonly 'also') in the normal mid position after the modal verb. ► adverbs
example_sentence: I must also do the FCE practice test because I will have the FCE test soon.

learning_objective: Can use ellipted 'must' without a subject. ► ellipsis
example_sentence: [at the end of a letter] Must go now.

learning_objective: Can use 'must' with the following verb ellipted where the previous main verb is understood.    ► ellipsis
example_sentence: You must! It's amazing!

learning_objective: Can use 'must' to guess and deduce, most commonly with 'it must be', or 'you must be'.
example_sentence: Second, it must be boring for you to sit at a computer all day.

learning_objective: Can use 'must' (with a wide range of pronouns and nouns) to talk about obligation and necessity.
example_sentence: [talking about something the speaker has found] She took it and said we must go and find him.

learning_objective: Can use 'must' to make strong, encouraging suggestions.
example_sentence: This club has been opened for just two weeks. I mean we must see it.

learning_objective: Can use the affirmative form.
example_sentence: I think you ought to go on holiday with both of them.

learning_objective: Can use 'ought to' with 'you' to give advice.
example_sentence: Then, if you'd rather go with your friend, you ought to talk to your parents first, and tell them what you think about it.

learning_objective: Can use 'shall' to talk about plans and intentions, particularly in formal contexts.
example_sentence: I promise that I shall cover all the work and give it to you tomorrow.

learning_objective: Can use 'shall' to seek advice.
example_sentence: What shall I buy for her wedding?

learning_objective: Can use 'should have' + '-ed'.
example_sentence: I have an appointment. I know I shouldn't have taken it during school time but I had no other choice.

learning_objective: Can use 'should' have + '-ed'.
example_sentence: You should have been with us.

learning_objective: Can use the question tag form 'shouldn't' + pronoun to make suggestions and ask for opinions.
example_sentence: When you come back, we should rent some movies, shouldn't we?

learning_objective: Can use 'should' to talk about what is likely to happen.
example_sentence: [talking about an event] It should be a good day!

learning_objective: Can use 'should' to talk about ideal or desired situations.
example_sentence: British people should eat more vegetables and fruit and avoid fast food!

learning_objective: Can use 'you shouldn’t have' (+ '-ed') in polite acceptance of a gift.
example_sentence: Thank you for the present you sent me, you shouldn't have!

learning_objective: Can use 'you should have' + '-ed' to emphasise points in a story.
example_sentence: You should have heard the noise.

learning_objective: Can use 'should have' + '-ed' to talk about regrets or undesirable situations in the past.
example_sentence: You should have been there with me, I had a great time.

learning_objective: Can use 'used to' to talk about repeated actions or states in the past that are no longer true.
example_sentence: When I was I child, I used to go to a small school and I enjoyed it very much.

learning_objective: Can use the affirmative form.
example_sentence: I used to share my room with my brother.

learning_objective: Can use the negative forms 'didn't use to' and 'didn't used to'.
example_sentence: We didn't use to talk to each other.

learning_objective: Can use 'will' to talk about fixed plans in the future, often with timetabled times or dates.
example_sentence: Mr Miller, the Sales Director, will visit us next Thursday.

learning_objective: Can use 'will' to make predictions.► question tags
example_sentence: I think that in 20 years' time it will be bigger than it is, because my town is growing very fast.

learning_objective: Can use 'would not have' + '-ed' or 'wouldn’t have' + '-ed'
example_sentence: Without your help, I wouldn't have got this result.

learning_objective: Can use 'would have' + '-ed'.
example_sentence: I did not think my friend Janis would have done that.

learning_objective: Can use 'would' in the main clause of a conditional sentence to talk about an imagined situation, often in the context of advice or opinion-giving.
example_sentence: It's your decision but if I had the chance, I would go with my friends.

learning_objective: Can use 'would' to talk about the future in the past.
example_sentence: I've just bought a new dress, and a beautiful black T-shirt. When I saw them, I thought they would be great for tonight's party.

learning_objective: Can use an limited range of adverbs with 'would', including 'really', 'probably', 'certainly', 'definitely'.► adverbs
example_sentence: I hope you will come soon, and I think that you would really enjoy it because is something different of what you are used to.

learning_objective: Can use question forms.
example_sentence: Would you let me know what you think?

learning_objective: Can use 'would' to make polite requests, often in the fixed expression 'would you mind'.
example_sentence: Would you tell me about your room in your next letter, please?

learning_objective: Can use 'would' with verbs such as 'advise', 'imagine', 'recommend', 'say' to be less direct.
example_sentence: I'd advise you to go with your friends because I bet you'll have so much fun!

guideword: USE: IMAGINED SITUATIONS IN THE PAST
learning_objective: Can use 'would' to talk about imagined situations in the past. ► conditionals
example_sentence: The next week I would have flown to Miami to meet George Clooney.

learning_objective: Can use 'would' as the past form of 'will' in reported clauses.
example_sentence: She accepted the invitation and she said that she would come on the 21st of June.

learning_objective: Can use the negative forms of 'would' to talk about willingness in the past.
example_sentence: We had to leave without you because the bus driver wouldn't wait.

learning_objective: Can form negative statements of main verbs in the past continuous and past perfect with auxiliary verbs 'be' and 'have' + 'not/n't'. ► past continuous ► past perfect
example_sentence: It was raining for seventy two hours and it wasn't going to stop.

learning_objective: Can use negative question forms in main clauses and question tags. ► questions
example_sentence: Can't you go with your parents some time and with your friends later?

learning_objective: Can use the negative forms of mental process verbs ('I don’t think', 'I don’t believe') followed by a complement clause, where the negative form is in the mental process verb rather than the complement clause.
example_sentence: But I don't think it'll help you.

guideword: FORM: 'NOT', NON FINITE AND ELLIPTED CLAUSES
learning_objective: Can use 'not' before a non-finite or ellipted clause.
example_sentence: But every time a small envelope arrived, I ripped it open as fast as I could. Not this time.

learning_objective: Can use negative questions and question tags to involve the listener/reader by seeking agreement or checking something.
example_sentence: Isn't it marvellous?

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of indefinite pronouns in negative contexts.
example_sentence: I said I had nothing, but he didn't believe me.

learning_objective: Can use adverbs 'ever', 'never', 'yet', 'still' in negative contexts.
example_sentence: Nobody will ever know who that man was.

guideword: FORM: COMPLEX NOUN PHRASES WITH ADVERBS + ADJECTIVES
learning_objective: Can form complex noun phrases with adverb + adjective + noun.
example_sentence: It took us a very long time but it was a great fun!

learning_objective: Can form complex noun phrases by using a prepositional phrase after the main noun in a noun phrase.
example_sentence: It was a beautiful red dress with blue flowers on the back.

guideword: FORM: COMPLEX NOUN PHRASES WITH ADJECTIVES
learning_objective: Can form an increasing range of complex noun phrases with more than one adjective.
example_sentence: The Northeast is so beautiful and it's famous because of its white sandy beaches and the coconut trees.

learning_objective: Can form noun phrases with 'such a' + adjective + singular noun and 'such' + adjective + plural noun, to give emphasis.
example_sentence: It was so terrible because I've never been in such a terrible situation.

learning_objective: Can use a clause with a present perfect or past perfect as a superlative noun phrase complement, to talk about uniqueness.
example_sentence: It has the best promenade I have seen.

learning_objective: Can omit the noun after possessive '’s' to talk about a place which is familiar to the speaker and listener.
example_sentence: Last week I left my clothes at the dry cleaner's and I can't collect them.

learning_objective: Can put two noun phrases together (in apposition) to refer to the same person or thing, usually separated by commas.
example_sentence: Mr Miller, the Sales Director, will visit us next Thursday.

learning_objective: Can use the noun phrase 'The thing' in front position, with a clause complement, to give focus to something.
example_sentence: The thing I enjoyed most about it was a new computer game. (Georgia (Republic of); B1 THRESHOLD; 2007; Georgian; Pass)

guideword: FORM: COMPLEX NOUN PHRASE WITH POSSESSIVE '‘S' + NOUN
learning_objective: Can form complex noun phrases with noun phrase + 'of' + noun phrase + possessive determiner ''s' + noun phrase.
example_sentence: I ran to the street and I saw the window of my neighbour's car was broken.

learning_objective: Can form noun phrases with an indefinite pronoun + adjective or clause.
example_sentence: We see each other every day and do something different every day.

learning_objective: Can form noun phrases with 'a friend of' or 'friends of' + possessive determiner + noun.
example_sentence: He is a friend of my husband.

learning_objective: Can form noun phrases with noun + 'of' + possessive pronouns.
example_sentence: … we always did everything together and couldn't stop laughing as she told me many funny stories and I told her lots of silly mistakes of mine.

learning_objective: Can use collective nouns, usually with a singular verb, but also with a plural verb depending on whether the noun is perceived as a single group or collection of individuals.
example_sentence: The staff is friendly and polite with guests and there are many sports activities.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of common nouns.
example_sentence: When you're swimming you exercise all of your body.

learning_objective: Can use uncountable nouns with an increasing range of quantity words and phrases including 'much', 'a bit of', 'a little bit of', 'enough', 'further', 'plenty of', 'loads of'.  ►  Determiners: quantity
example_sentence: I'm sure that your parents are looking forward to going with you to the beach, because they work a lot of hours during the year so they can't spend much time with you.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of uncountable nouns.
example_sentence: I think the countryside is better than the town centre, people get on more easily with each other.

learning_objective: Can form the 'get'-passive with a range of forms of 'get' + past participles.
example_sentence: [talking about a mirror] I bought it because mine got broken, so I need it to see myself in it.

guideword: FORM/USE: WITH 'BY' IN A RELATIVE CLAUSE|
learning_objective: Can use the passive with 'by' in a relative clause, often to add more information.
example_sentence: I also like wearing clothes which are manufactured by famous brands.

learning_objective: Can use the present simple passive affirmative with a range of pronoun and noun subjects.
example_sentence: Our office is situated near the airport.

learning_objective: Can use the past simple passive affirmative with a range of pronoun and noun subjects both singular and plural.
example_sentence: It was written in a strange language that I tried to translate.

learning_objective: Can use the present continuous passive affirmative with a limited range of verbs.
example_sentence: Did you know that the next Harry Potter movie is being filmed in my school?

learning_objective: Can use the passive infinitive after a limited number of forms including 'going to', 'have to', 'need to', 'want to'.
example_sentence: It is going to be shown this Friday.

learning_objective: Can use the present continuous passive to refer to the future.
example_sentence: The seminar is being held at Chennai in the next week.

learning_objective: Can use the passive with 'by' to give focus.
example_sentence: My school was chosen by the TV company because it is one of the newest in town.

guideword: FORM: WITH VERBS TAKING TWO OBJECTS.
learning_objective: Can use the past simple passive with a limited range of verbs needing two objects, putting the indirect object in subject position.
example_sentence: So I was given a ticket for a train, running from Berlin to Munich.

learning_objective: Can use the present simple passive negative with a range of pronoun and noun subjects.
example_sentence: The story is about a girl, who is not accepted in her class.

learning_objective: Can use the question form.
example_sentence: Was my mother going to die?

learning_objective: Can use the past continuous with an increasing range of adverbs in the normal mid position.
example_sentence: He was still waiting at the traffic light.

learning_objective: Can use the past continuous to give a reason for something, often with 'because'.
example_sentence: Because she was sitting in the corner, she could see the whole view of the café in front of her.

learning_objective: Can use the negative form.
example_sentence: He was not planning to become a professional player.

learning_objective: Can use the past continuous to talk about ongoing repeated events in the past, often with 'always'.
example_sentence: Worst of all, in September it was always raining.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect continuous talk about a background action or event which began before a point in the past and was still continuing up to a point in the past when the main event happened.
example_sentence: I had been waiting for this letter for months, so I opened it immediately.

learning_objective: Can use the affirmative form.
example_sentence: I had been working on the project for over a year.

guideword: USE: CONTINUING EVENTS IN THE PAST
learning_objective: Can use the past perfect continuous to talk about an action or event which began before a point in the past and was still continuing up to that point, often with 'for' or 'since', to give background information.
example_sentence: I know it very well because we had been exchanging letters for a long time.

learning_objective: Can use negative forms.
example_sentence: The ABC Company called this morning to say that our company had not delivered the goods on time.

learning_objective: Can use the affirmative form.
example_sentence: I felt really sorry for you after I had read your last letter.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect simple with a limited range of adverbs (including 'never', 'ever', 'just', 'always', 'already') in the normal mid-position. ► adverbs
example_sentence: It was the most beautiful one I had ever seen.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect simple in 'if'-clauses to talk about imagined situations in the past, often with regret. ► 'if'-clauses
example_sentence: If the weather had been better, we would have enjoyed it more.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect simple to talk about a time before another time in the past.
example_sentence: He then thought how stupid he had been.

learning_objective: Can use the affirmative form with a wide range of verbs.
example_sentence: I convinced some of my friends to come with me even if they don't like rap music.

learning_objective: Can use the negative form with an increasing range of verbs.
example_sentence: I didn't realise I had lost the ring till I was at home.

learning_objective: Can use the past simple to talk about habitual states or actions.
example_sentence: … he always seemed so confident to me!

learning_objective: Can use the past simple to order sequences of events in the past, in the context of narratives.
example_sentence: I remember her as a shy girl, but I read she became a manager, she went on lots of trips, she met a lot of new friends, she got married and then she got divorced.

learning_objective: Can use the past simple with 'wish (that)' to express regret that things are not different.
example_sentence: I wish that you were here, cycling with us.

learning_objective: Can use the past simple after 'if' to talk about the possible result of an imagined situation in the present or future.
example_sentence: It would be better if you spent some time in the fresh air with your friends or walking in a park.

learning_objective: Can use 'yes/no', 'wh-', tag and negative question forms with a limited range of verbs.
example_sentence: Did you receive my present?

learning_objective: Can use the question form with a limited range of verbs.
example_sentence: Have you been thinking about it a lot?!

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect continuous to talk about repeated activities which began in the past and are still continuing.
example_sentence: I've been watching TV a lot, specially the sports programmes.

learning_objective: Can use the affirmative form.
example_sentence: I have been thinking about your letter.

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect continuous to talk about a single activity or state that began in the past and is still continuing, often with 'for' or 'since'.
example_sentence: I've been studying International business there for four years.

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect simple with 'already' to emphasise that something is done, often before the expected time.  ► adverbs
example_sentence: I've already planned the party, bought his present, and most important of all, I 've already told everyone.

learning_objective: Can use the affirmative forms with a range of pronouns and nouns and an increasing range of verbs.
example_sentence: I've left some clothes at the dry cleaner's 100 yards from my home, in Park Street.

learning_objective: Can use the negative forms with a range of pronouns and an increasing range of verbs.
example_sentence: He hasn't spoken to anyone from that time till now.

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect simple with 'since' to talk about duration.
example_sentence: I have been here since August 2006.

learning_objective: Can use the negative question form.
example_sentence: Haven't you seen it yet?

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect simple to refer to a finished event or state in the recent past, that has a present relevance, often with 'just'.  ► adverbs
example_sentence: My mother has broken her right leg.

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect simple with an increasing range of adverbs in the normal mid position.
example_sentence: This is a famous seafood restaurant, you know, I've always loved seafood.

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect simple to refer to a state or period of time which is unlimited or indefinite.
example_sentence: In spite of bad weather forecasts on TV, it has been a sunny, warm weekend.

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect simple with a superlative adjective to talk about a unique experience.  ► adjectives: superlatives
example_sentence: This restaurant has the most delicious shrimps I have ever tasted.

learning_objective: Can use a wide range of simple (single word) prepositions.
example_sentence: If it is raining, the rain enters through the holes in the roof and makes the house wet.

learning_objective: Can end a reported question clause and a relative clause with a preposition, where the preposition is stranded from its complement. ► Clauses: relative
example_sentence: So this is the best festival I been to.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of complex prepositions.
example_sentence: My parents weren't happy when they heard that I wanted to go to Croatia with my friends instead of flying to the USA.

learning_objective: Can use adverbs of degree to modify prepositions and prepositional phrases.
example_sentence: It's quite near my house so I can go there easily.

learning_objective: Can use the present continuous to describe temporary actions which are repeated or regular.
example_sentence: I'm doing a lot of great leisure activities.

learning_objective: Can use the present continuous with a wide range of verbs to talk about temporary situations.
example_sentence: I'm enjoying a sunny day, much better than England.

learning_objective: Can use the present continuous with a wide range of verbs to talk about situations and events in progress.
example_sentence: I’m planning a picnic on Saturday in the park.

learning_objective: Can use 'don't you think' or 'don't you agree' to look for agreement or an opinion.
example_sentence: It's a bit strange, don't you think?

learning_objective: Can use 'yes/no' and 'wh-' question forms with a wide range of subjects.
example_sentence: Does your brother go with your parents?

learning_objective: Can use the present simple with an increasing a range of mental process verbs, including 'remember', 'understand', 'believe'.
example_sentence: I'm reading an adventure book, I don't remember the title but it's very boring!

learning_objective: Can use the present simple with a limited range of speech act verbs, including 'suggest', 'apologise', 'recommend'.
example_sentence: I suggest that if you travel, you should pay for your trip.

learning_objective: Can use the present simple with a limited range of reporting verbs, including 'say', 'show'.
example_sentence: For example, I know a person that moved to my city two years ago, and now he says that it improved his social life.

learning_objective: Can use 'this' as a pronoun to refer back to whole clauses or sentences, especially to highlight or continue an important topic.
example_sentence: To stay fit I'm eating lots of fruit and vegetables, but meat and fish, too. I think you should do this, too, if you haven't done it in the past.

learning_objective: Can use 'those' as a pronoun with plural reference.  ►  noun phrases
example_sentence: Most of my clothes are lemon, green or orange, those are my favourite colours!

learning_objective: Can use 'those' as a pronoun to refer to things which have already been mentioned.  ►  noun phrases
example_sentence: Those are the things I like the most about her.

learning_objective: Can use 'these' as a pronoun with plural reference.  ►  noun phrases
example_sentence: These are the most important years for us as teenagers, and they have to be the best years!

learning_objective: Can use 'these' as a pronoun to refer to something with immediate relevance which has already been mentioned.  ►  noun phrases  ►  pronouns: demonstrative
example_sentence: He is very clever and generous, and these are the things that I like most about him.

learning_objective: Can use 'this one', 'that one' as a substitute for countable singular nouns that have previously been mentioned.
example_sentence: I don't usually wear dresses but I had to buy this one because I am going to be in a singing competition.

learning_objective: Can use quantifying determiners + 'of' with demonstrative pronouns  ► determiners: demonstrative
example_sentence: If you can't, you must choose one of these.

learning_objective: Can use 'one' to refer to people in general, in formal contexts.
example_sentence: The television, I think, is like a box where one can find anything: rubbish and good things.

learning_objective: Can use a wide range of indefinite pronouns as objects or complements.
example_sentence: I often watch TV, because my computer is broken and I have nothing to do. (Georgia (Republic of); B1 THRESHOLD; 2007; Georgian; Pass)

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of indefinite pronouns ('something', 'nobody') as subjects, with a singular verb.
example_sentence: I was so confused that I thought something was happening.

learning_objective: Can use premodifiers with indefinite pronouns, often to intensify.
example_sentence: You can find absolutely anything, whatever you need.

learning_objective: Can use the possessive pronoun 'yours' after noun + 'of'.
example_sentence: Maybe you can take a friend of yours with you there.

learning_objective: Can use the possessive pronouns 'mine' and 'yours', with singular reference, in comparative clauses after '(not) as … as'.
example_sentence: Hope you have a friend as good as mine!

learning_objective: Can use the possessive pronoun 'yours', with singular reference, in subject position.
example_sentence: We bought it because yours is very old and sometimes stops working.

guideword: FORM: SUBJECT AND OBJECT PRONOUNS, 'BOTH', 'A FEW', 'ANOTHER'
learning_objective: Can use 'both', 'a few', 'another' as subject and object pronouns.
example_sentence: [talking about two types of exercise] Both are going to keep you fit.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of pronouns ('some', 'a few', 'any', 'each') with 'of' followed by an object pronoun.
example_sentence: Of course some of them are closer than others but I love them all.

learning_objective: Can use 'each other' as the object of a verb or complement of a preposition to talk about the mutual behaviour of two or more people.
example_sentence: We go to the same school so we see each other every day.

guideword: FORM: SAME SUBJECT AND OBJECT, SINGULAR
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of singular reflexive pronouns with an increasing range of verbs to refer to actions where the subject and object of the verb are the same.
example_sentence: I decided to talk to him and introduce myself.

learning_objective: Can use the singular reflexive pronouns 'myself', 'yourself', 'himself' and 'herself' with 'by' to mean alone, without any help.
example_sentence: I will ask my friends from the class and I will study by myself.

learning_objective: Can use the singular reflexive pronouns 'myself', 'yourself', 'himself' and 'herself' after prepositions where the object of the preposition is the same as the subject of the verb.
example_sentence: He told me about himself and how he started playing the guitar.

learning_objective: Can use the singular reflexive pronouns 'myself', 'yourself', 'himself' and 'herself' for emphasis.
example_sentence: The best solution will be if you decide yourself what to do.

learning_objective: Can use dummy 'it' as a subject to introduce something the speaker or writer is going to refer to.
example_sentence: It is best to spend your time in the countryside.

learning_objective: Can use 'this one', 'that one' to refer to a singular countable noun.  ►  pronouns: demonstratives
example_sentence: I met many old friends there, because I was in another judo club before this one and my friends and I left the old one to join the 1.JCF, because we heard that this one is the best in the whole of Frankfurt.

learning_objective: Can use 'one' after 'which' in indirect questions to refer to one of two or more options.
example_sentence: If you ask me which one I would choose, I would first go camping and then to Disneyland in Paris.

learning_objective: Can use 'ones' as a pronoun to substitute for plural nouns which have already been mentioned or are obvious from the context.
example_sentence: My pillows are getting old and I need new ones.

learning_objective: Can use premodifiers with 'ones', to refer to something in general.
example_sentence: Our computers are out-dated and slow. I think we should replace them with new ones.

learning_objective: Can use determiners + premodifers + 'ones'.
example_sentence: We should buy some new copiers and some new faxes because the old ones are very slow, and one copier is already broken.

learning_objective: Can use 'the ones' with a complement, to refer to something specific.
example_sentence: Weddings in our country? Well, they're pretty different from the ones in England.

learning_objective: Can form alternative question with 'or something else' as the second alternative to a noun phrase, to refer to something non-specific.
example_sentence: Shall we buy him a book, a CD or something else?

learning_objective: Can form alternative questions using 'or not' to substitute for a clause, sometimes to express annoyance or impatience.
example_sentence: For a few seconds he was quiet. I couldn't wait, I asked nervously about his exam, did he pass or not?

learning_objective: Can form alternative questions with two phrases combined with 'or'.
example_sentence: Is this area in the city or in the countryside?

learning_objective: Can form alternative questions with two clauses combined with 'or'.
example_sentence: Are you still with him or did you two break up?

guideword: FORM: AFFIRMATIVE MAIN CLAUSES + AFFIRMATIVE QUESTION TAGS
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of affirmative tags with affirmative clauses.
example_sentence: Your boyfriend is also 16, is he?

guideword: FORM: AFFIRMATIVE MAIN CLAUSES + NEGATIVE QUESTION TAGS
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of 'be', 'do', 'have' and modal verb tags with negative main clauses.
example_sentence: And so you're moving to a different area, aren't you?

guideword: FORM: NEGATIVE MAIN CLAUSES + AFFIRMATIVE QUESTION TAGS
learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of affirmative 'be', 'do' and 'have' tags with negative main clauses.
example_sentence: I mean, your parents don't like discos, do they?

learning_objective: Can use 'right' as a tag in informal contexts.
example_sentence: You are 14 years old, right?

learning_objective: Can use 'wh-'words + the negative form of modal verbs + subject + main verb to form 'wh-'questions.
example_sentence: Why can't you do both things?

learning_objective: Can use main verb 'be' + 'n’t' to form negative 'yes/no' questions.
example_sentence: Aren't you excited about it?

learning_objective: Can use auxiliary 'do' and 'have' + 'n’t' + subject + main verb to form negative 'yes/no' questions.
example_sentence: Don't you think so?

learning_objective: Can use modal verbs + 'not' + subject + main verb to form 'yes/no' questions.
example_sentence: Wouldn't you like to come?

learning_objective: Can use negative 'yes/no' questions to involve the listener or reader by seeking agreement.
example_sentence: Finally two days ago I found it in my pocket! It's funny! Don't you think?

learning_objective: Can report questions using 'ask' + 'if' or 'whether' + clause, with a pronoun and tense shift where relevant.
example_sentence: You asked me if I like shopping.

learning_objective: Can report 'wh-'questions using a reporting verb + 'wh-'word + clause, with a change of pronoun and tense shift where relevant.
example_sentence: I asked why she was here.

guideword: FORM: REPORTED STATEMENTS, PRONOUN AND TENSE SHIFT
learning_objective: Can report statements using a reporting clause with 'say' or 'tell' + 'that-'clause, with a pronoun and tense shift where relevant.
example_sentence: I immediately called her to see if everything was o.k. and she said that she had won the lottery and she wanted to give some of the money to me.

learning_objective: Can report speech about events in the future with and without backshifts for words relating to time.
example_sentence: At the airport they told him to come the next morning.

learning_objective: Can report requests and commands with 'ask' or 'tell' + direct object and 'to-'infinitive.
example_sentence: She asked me to come back as soon as possible because my father is seriously ill.

guideword: FORM: DIRECT SPEECH, REPORTING CLAUSE, END POSITION
learning_objective: Can report speech and thought directly using the reporting verb after the reporting clause.
example_sentence: “Excuse me Sir, but you forgot your mobile phone," she said.

guideword: FORM: DIRECT SPEECH, REPORTING CLAUSE, INITIAL POSITION
learning_objective: Can report speech and thought directly, using the reporting verb before the reporting clause.
example_sentence: He felt like a worm too and he said to me: "I'm sorry I'm not a good friend".

learning_objective: Can report thought using 'wonder' + 'wh-'word + clause, with a tense shift where relevant.
example_sentence: I always had good times with her and was very impatient to read this letter: I wondered what she had written.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of verbs, typically reporting or mental process verbs, with a 'that'-clause as the direct object.
example_sentence: Now you're sixteen, I totally understand that you don't want to go on holiday with your parents.

learning_objective: Can use some reporting verbs with a prepositional phrase followed by a 'that'-clause as the direct object.
example_sentence: I think also that you should say to your parents that you want to try a new experience with your friends.

learning_objective: Can use a wide range of verbs followed by a 'to-' infinitive.
example_sentence: … it's one of the reasons I decided to change class.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of verbs followed by an '-ing' form.
example_sentence: I would like to stay there a few years to finish studying and to have my own money to start thinking of travelling.

learning_objective: Can use 'make' and 'let' + infinitive without 'to'.
example_sentence: Let me know if it is suitable for you.

learning_objective: Can use 'would prefer' + 'to' infinitive, often for politeness.
example_sentence: I would prefer to pay by cash.

learning_objective: Can use 'help' + object + infinitive with or without 'to', with no change in meaning.
example_sentence: I wanted to help him to make some friends. He's just like me.

learning_objective: Can use some verbs of requesting and commanding followed by a direct object and a 'to-'infinitive.
example_sentence: I would like you to come to a picnic on Saturday.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of phrasal verbs + object pronoun + particle.
example_sentence: I heard about this club when I was watching T.V. and I wanted to check it out.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of phrasal verbs + particle + object.
example_sentence: Were it not for my university, I would give up this class immediately.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of phrasal verbs without an object.
example_sentence: I needed to hang out because my life was too jam-packed and hectic.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of verbs + particle + preposition + noun or pronoun.
example_sentence: I could catch up with the work I miss after school from 3 to 4.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of prepositional verbs followed by noun or pronoun objects.
example_sentence: If you need more information, please look at the information sheet at the entrance.

learning_objective: Can use 'there' with a range of modal verbs + 'be' + complement.
example_sentence: [talking about 20 years in the future] There will be flying cars and buses, and big houses.

learning_objective: Can use 'There' + 'be' + 'n’t' with countable and uncountable nouns.
example_sentence: There isn't any noise.

guideword: FORM: SEMI-MODAL AUXILIARY VERBS, 'USED TO', 'OUGHT TO'
learning_objective: Can use semi-modal auxiliary verbs, 'used to' and 'ought to'.
example_sentence: But we used to play together every single day!

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of compound adjectives ('up-to-date', 'state-of-the-art')
example_sentence: The selling point is that it contains the most up-to-date and state-of-the-art know-how, for instance a water-proof keyboard, information security system, etc. A survey of our customers last year indicated that over 70% of them are content with the user-friendly designs.

learning_objective: Can use adjective phrases to modify nouns.
example_sentence: Besides, we could leave one day in the morning and travel by boat to a small and exotic island full of different and exotic species of birds that we could observe and photograph.

learning_objective: Can use 'slightly' to modify comparative adjectives to a small degree.
example_sentence: I bought a new one, since the price of a second-hand one was slightly cheaper.

learning_objective: Can use 'a lot' to modify and intensify comparative adjectives used predicatively after a verb, usually 'be'.
example_sentence: In addition, remember that a bike, even a good one, is a lot cheaper than a car.

learning_objective: Can use 'much' to modify and intensify comparative adjectives used attributively before countable and uncountable nouns.
example_sentence: Although it is a second-hand computer which one of my colleagues sold me, it is a much better investment than the bicycle, I think.

learning_objective: Can use 'enough' after adjectives followed by 'to'-infinitive
example_sentence: [talking about an arts festival] I was lucky enough to take part in it last November, and I enjoyed it immensely.

learning_objective: Can form adjective phrases with 'rather a' + adjective, often in formal contexts.
example_sentence: Films tend to be rather a simple art, while books are a thing to be enjoyed in a calmer way.
