learning_objective: Can use the adjectives 'present', 'future', 'former' before a noun.
example_sentence: I think we all are very concerned about the present situation.

learning_objective: Can use a limited range of degree adjectives ('real', 'absolute', 'complete') before a noun to express intensity.
example_sentence: When tourists arrive, there is no area to park. This is a real problem and our town is becoming very dangerous for children; every week there is an accident.

learning_objective: Can use the premodifer 'by far' to make a superlative adjective stronger.
example_sentence: As you know, I've already visited almost fifty forests all over the world but I think this one is by far the most beautiful of all.

learning_objective: Can use '(one of) the' with an increasing range of superlative adjectives without a following noun, when the noun is understood.
example_sentence: There have been so many embarrassing moments in my life. It's very difficult to pick the most embarrassing.

learning_objective: Can use superlative adjectives before a noun + 'to'-infinitive. ► clauses: comparative
example_sentence: To be honest, I think that, to be in competition with other markets, we need to be updated and the easiest way to do it is to use computers.

learning_objective: Can modify an adverb with a comparative structure.
example_sentence: Obviously, you can get to work by car quicker and more comfortably than by bicycle.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use a wide range of manner adverbs to modify how something happpens.
example_sentence: She realises that he is a very dangerous man and that she should take his warning seriously.

sub_category: adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings
learning_objective: Can use a wide range of adverbs and adverb phrases to refer to the timing of an event.
example_sentence: I look forward hearing from you shortly and I thank you in advance.

learning_objective: Can use degree adverbs ('almost', 'very') to modify determiners. ► determiners
example_sentence: Today, thanks to the Internet, I can work at home almost all the time, which is far more relaxing and less boring.

learning_objective: Can use degree adverbs to modify pronouns. ► pronouns
example_sentence: You can eat very well and pay almost nothing.

learning_objective: Can use degree adverbs to modify noun phrases. ► noun phrases
example_sentence: It would be quite a formal meeting.

learning_objective: Can use 'never' in front position followed by an inverted subject (most commonly 'I') + main verb verb, to give focus.
example_sentence: Never have I seen such a realistic and impressive film before.

learning_objective: Can use 'the same' (+ noun) + 'as' + clause.
example_sentence: Maybe I'll do the same thing as you did.

learning_objective: Can use 'enough' after adjectives followed by 'to'-infinitive.
example_sentence: In my opinion young people at that age are not mature enough to decide by themselves what they want for their future.

learning_objective: Can use a superlative adjective (+ noun) (+ 'that') followed by a clause to talk about something unique.
example_sentence: It was probably the best comedy that anyone could ever have made; however, to the ones who were in the cinema, it didn't seem like a comedy but a horror film.

learning_objective: .||Can use than + an increasing range of non-finite clauses (to-infinitive, reduced clause)  forming the second part of a comparison after a comparative adjective||
example_sentence: It is much easier to be single than to have a partner.

learning_objective: Can use 'rather than' + a non-finite clause to stress one element in a comparison.
example_sentence: If you prefer watching TV rather than reading, you have to be critical: you mustn't in any way accept everything, every image that passes in front of your eyes.

guideword: FORM: 'AS IF' OR 'AS THOUGH' + FINITE CLAUSE
learning_objective: Can use 'as if' or 'as though' + finite clause to introduce a second clause as a comparison.
example_sentence: Her heart beat extremely fast, as if it was going to burst out of her chest.

learning_objective: Can use conditional subordinate clauses with a range of conjunctions ('as long as', 'provided') + present simple or present continuous, with future reference.
example_sentence: As long as it involves children I'd be delighted to work for you during the summer.

learning_objective: Can combine two main clauses of the same type using 'neither' in front or mid position, followed by 'nor' to introduce the second clause, for emphasis.
example_sentence: We neither walk nor do exercises.

learning_objective: Can combine clauses using 'not only' in mid position of the first clause followed by … 'but (also)' to introduce the second clause, to give focus.
example_sentence: The atmosphere was friendly, not only because it wasn't too crowded, but also because the waiters always help you.

learning_objective: Can use 'let's not' + base form of a main verb to make a suggestion.
example_sentence: Let's not lose track of each other again!

learning_objective: Can use 'do not' for emphasis or in formal contexts.
example_sentence: Do not forget to have a break at lunchtime and try to eat your meal whenever possible in a silent room.

learning_objective: Can form negative interrogative clauses with an adverb in mid position, between the subject and the main verb.
example_sentence: Money - hasn't it always been one of the most important problems for students?

learning_objective: Can use the negative interrogative form of an auxiliary verb to express surprise or enthusiasm.
example_sentence: Wouldn't it be wonderful!

learning_objective: Can use defining relative clauses and non-defining relative clauses ending in a preposition, with 'who/which/that' as the complement of the preposition.
example_sentence: Thank you very much for having provided us with valuable information about your finance services, which we are greatly interested in.

learning_objective: Can use non-defining relative clauses with 'whose', to give additional information.
example_sentence: And I was initially employed to be an entrance assistant, whose job was to keep the queue under control as well as keeping the area clean.

learning_objective: Can use defining relative clauses with 'whose', to give more information about relationship.
example_sentence: I visited several primary schools in Canberra and had opportunities to teach not only Australian children but also Vietnamese children whose parents immigrated to Australia.

learning_objective: Can use a relative clause to refer to a whole clause or sentence, often to express an opinion or evaluation or give a reason.
example_sentence: It is now possible to send a letter and receive an answer within ten minutes, which is a good thing if you want to buy a house, a car etc.

learning_objective: Can use a non-finite subordinate clause with 'after' + 'having/being' + '-ed' form, before a main clause, to refer to past time.
example_sentence: After having worked for many hours, you'll probably be tired.

learning_objective: Can use a finite subordinate clause, before or after a main clause, with conjunctions to introduce conditions. ► clauses: conditionals
example_sentence: Unless something is done many people will die.

learning_objective: Can use a non-finite clause, introduced by '-ing' before a main clause, to give more information.
example_sentence: Having no choice, I sat down on a bench which was in the middle of the road to wait for the rain to stop.

guideword: FORM/USE: 'BOTH … AND' WITH PHRASES AND CLAUSES
learning_objective: Can use 'both … and' to combine phrases and clauses, often for emphasis.
example_sentence: And we have won a high reputation both at home and abroad.

learning_objective: Can use 'neither … nor' to connect two words, phrases or clauses, often to give emphasis or focus.
example_sentence: Neither you nor I would like that.

learning_objective: Can use more complex subordinating conjunctions ('as long as, as soon as, in order that, despite the fact that, due to the fact that, as if, as though') to introduce a subordinate clause.
example_sentence: As far as I am concerned, keeping animals in zoos is not cruel as long as the zoos keep to certain rules concerning the conditions the animals have to be kept in.

learning_objective: Can use a wide range of simple subordinating conjunctions ('once, whereas, unless, except (that) provided (that)'), to introduce a subordinate clause.
example_sentence: Believe me, once you have tried it, you'll never give it up.

learning_objective: Can use the in comparative phrases 'more', 'less', 'worse', 'better … the more', 'less', 'worse …' to talk about one thing that is affected by another. ► comparative clauses
example_sentence: [talking about how watching TV is bad for you] I mean that you can watch one or two hours a day but the more you watch, the worse you become.

learning_objective: Can use ''s' after a noun phrase with of to indicate possession.
example_sentence: Actually, I know a great one and they can make a little discount for us because the owner is a friend of my father's.

learning_objective: Can use 's'' after plural nouns to indicate possession.
example_sentence: I decided to spend the rest of my holiday in my parents' house, near the sea.

learning_objective: Can use 'their' before nouns to refer to a generic body or group of people expressed as a singular subject. ► generic pronouns
example_sentence: … yet everyone has their preference for one or another.

learning_objective: Can use possessive determiner 'its' before nouns to refer to a singular inanimate subject or object.
example_sentence: It was the biggest TV in the bazaar with its huge, black screen.

learning_objective: Can use modifier + 'little' + uncountable nouns and modifier + 'few' + countable nouns, to indicate a lack of something or not as much as expected of something.► adverbs
example_sentence: I spend very little time outside, and I know I do not have enough stamina and strength.

learning_objective: Can use a range of phrases as discourse markers to introduce a comparison.
example_sentence: [talking about celebrating New Year] But some countries celebrate on other dates too, like they have Chinese New Year according to their calendar.  Likewise, in Nepal New Year is celebrated on 1st of Baishak, which is the start of a new year according to the Nepalese calendar.

learning_objective: Can use a range of phrases as discourse markers to summarise.
example_sentence: Thus, I would be very grateful if you would send me clear explanations of the following points: firstly, I would like you to specify what “the normal price” is, and therefore what would be the cost of two nights.

guideword: FORM/USE: ORGANISING, OPENINGS AND CLOSINGS, FORMAL
learning_objective: Can use a range of phrases as discourse markers to open and close texts and point to conclusions, often in formal contexts.
example_sentence: To begin with, I want to ensure that you are going to have a great time with us.

guideword: FORM/USE: 'THE THING/ FACT/ POINT/ PROBLEM IS (THAT)'
learning_objective: Can use 'The thing, fact, point, problem, or reason + is (that)' for focus.
example_sentence: The reason is I like children, I have experience of looking after disabled people and I like to do things like volunteering.

learning_objective: Can use 'The reason (that)', 'The place (which)' + clause as subject + 'be' for focus. ► Relative clauses
example_sentence: The reason I am writing is to suggest a few things about our holidays.

learning_objective: Can use the future continuous with 'will' as a polite question form, in place of the present simple or future simple.
example_sentence: Will I be needing any money?

learning_objective: Can use 'yes/no' and 'wh-' question forms with 'will'.
example_sentence: Will they be holding any activities at night?

learning_objective: Can use the present forms of 'be' + 'about to'.
example_sentence: You are not going to believe what I am about to tell you!

learning_objective: Can use 'be due to' and, more formally, 'be to' talk about things that are scheduled or expected.
example_sentence: Our annual conference for HR managers is due to take place on 10 December 2010.

learning_objective: Can use the present form of 'be' + 'to'.
example_sentence: Dear Erica, I am sorry that I cannot attend the meeting at head office as expected, as I am to go on a business trip to New York then.

guideword: USE: OBLIGATIONS AND  INSTRUCTIONS WITH 'BE TO'
learning_objective: Can use 'be to' talk about future obligations and to give instructions.
example_sentence: [talking about gorillas] We have no right to do so, because they are to be respected as much as any other living form on this planet!

guideword: USE: IMMEDIATE FUTURE WITH 'BE ABOUT TO'
learning_objective: Can use 'be about to' to talk about the immediate future, often with 'just'.
example_sentence: I know what you 're about to answer, and moreover you might be right;

learning_objective: Can use the present form of 'be' + 'due to'.
example_sentence: I am afraid that July is the only appropriate month, because I am due to start work in August.

learning_objective: Can use the simple past form of 'be' + 'on the point of' + '-ing' to talk about things that were expected to happen soon after a point in the past.
example_sentence: In addition, when I was on the point of leaving the office, another phone rang.

learning_objective: Can use the simple past form of 'be' + 'about to' (often with 'just') to talk about the immediate future from a point in the past.
example_sentence: Two days before, I told him that our father was about to buy a brand new car.

learning_objective: Can use the affirmative form with 'will'.
example_sentence: This summer I will have been working for three years for my company …

guideword: USE: LOOKING BACK FROM A POINT IN THE FUTURE
learning_objective: Can use the future perfect continuous to look back to the past from a point in the future and to emphasise the duration of an activity or event.
example_sentence: Now I am staying in Hastings in England and this month, I will have been studying English for seven months.

learning_objective: Can use the affirmative form with 'will'.
example_sentence: I'm sure I will have learned today's lesson by the next lesson.

learning_objective: Can use the negative form with 'will'.
example_sentence: I think that the best time for me to visit Scotland would be in July, because I will not have finished my university exams by June.

guideword: USE: EVENTS COMPLETED IN THE FUTURE
learning_objective: Can use the future perfect simple with 'will' to talk about something which is expected to be completed (or not completed) by a certain point in the future.
example_sentence: There is no problem if you want to leave at 4.30 pm as the match will have finished by that time.

learning_objective: Can use the present continuous with a wide range of verbs to talk about future arrangements.
example_sentence: We’re throwing a party for fun on 5th January.

learning_objective: Can use the present continuous with a wide range of common verbs to ask about future plans.
example_sentence: So are you planning to visit Japan then?

learning_objective: Can use 'it' + linking verb + adjective + ('that') clause.
example_sentence: It seems clear to me that swimming is good for the whole body.

guideword: FORM/USE: 'IT' + LINKING VERB + ADJECTIVES + CLAUSE, HEDGING
learning_objective: Can use 'it' + linking verb + adjective + ('that') clause to make an assertion less direct.
example_sentence: It does not seem possible that we can live without any private life.

guideword: FORM/USE: 'IT' + LINKING VERB + ADJECTIVES + ('THAT') CLAUSE, FOCUS
learning_objective: Can use 'it' + linking verb + adjective + ('that') clause as a focusing device.
example_sentence: It is clear that we can't live without computers today.

learning_objective: Can use pronoun or noun + 'be' + adjective + 'to' + (past) infinitive.
example_sentence: They are unlikely to arrive on Friday.

learning_objective: Can use 'can' with an increasing range of adverbs (including 'easily', 'hardly', 'now', 'probably', 'sometimes', 'still') in the normal mid position after the modal verb or after the subject in questions.  ► adverbs
example_sentence: In case you need them , I can easily provide references.

learning_objective: Can use 'can' to talk about general truths and tendencies.
example_sentence: …some young people can feel bored when they have to walk a lot and look at […] very old things.

learning_objective: Can use the negative forms of 'can't' and 'cannot' to guess, predict and deduce.
example_sentence: You look very pale and you can’t be feeling well.

learning_objective: Can use negative forms of 'can' to make reproaches or appeals.
example_sentence: You can’t forget your friends.

learning_objective: Can used 'could' have + '-ed'.
example_sentence: In fact it could have been dangerous but it became funny and also an embarrassing moment in my life!

learning_objective: Can used affirmative 'could' have + '-ed' to talk about past possibility.
example_sentence: Alice was disappointed that her mother could have phoned her but she did not.

learning_objective: Can use 'could have' + '-ed' to talk about regrets.
example_sentence: I wish I could have stayed, especially to help the children.

learning_objective: Can use 'could have' + '-ed' to speculate about the past.
example_sentence: I thought perhaps a thief could have entered my house.

learning_objective: Can use 'could' as the past form of 'can' in reported speech.
example_sentence: I told them I couldn't go because I didn't have money, I wasn't honest!

learning_objective: Can use negative form dare not and daren't +  infinitive without to.
example_sentence: [talking about a restaurant] But many local people dare not go to Lily, because it is too expensive.

learning_objective: Can use affirmative form dare + infinitive without to.
example_sentence: I can cook if you dare eat it!

learning_objective: Can use semi-modal 'dare' to talk about being brave enough to do something.
example_sentence: Some people object that I have to put up with the rain and other inconveniences, but I think that they are rather jealous because they daren't give up their car.

learning_objective: Can use the full range of expressions with 'be' + infinitive ('be likely to', 'be due to', 'be meant to', 'be bound to', 'be sure to', 'be certain to', 'be obliged to') with present and past forms of 'be' and with modal 'will' .
example_sentence: People who read books are likely to be more knowledgeable.

learning_objective: Can use 'be meant to' to talk about ideal states.
example_sentence: Animals are meant to be free, and man should be very careful about interfering with nature.

learning_objective: Can use 'be due to' and, more formally, 'be to' to talk about things that are scheduled or expected.
example_sentence: I have attached the … company law directive enacted 2 years ago and which is due to be implemented by 2008.

learning_objective: Can use 'be' (+modifier) 'likely to' to talk about probability.
example_sentence: And whenever money is involved, some problems are likely to happen.

learning_objective: Can use '(much) more/less' to modify 'likely' in a comparison.
example_sentence: In case of an accident, one is more likely to be badly injured when riding a bicycle.

learning_objective: Can use 'be supposed to' to talk about hearsay or supposition.
example_sentence: That woman is supposed to have killed her husbands (at least two), because she wanted their money.

learning_objective: Can use 'be' (+ modifier) 'bound to' to talk about something certain or inevitable.
example_sentence: I assure you that it is bound to be popular with different clients, especially young people and white-collar workers.

learning_objective: Can use 'be' + 'obvious/sure/likely/certain' followed by a 'that'-clause.
example_sentence: It is about 1000 years old, so I am sure that you will find it fascinating to see it.

learning_objective: Can use 'be supposed to' to talk about expectation or obligation.
example_sentence: However, the show is only on the 14th of March, the same day we are supposed to go to a Science Museum.

learning_objective: Can use the imperative form 'be sure to', to express obligation.
example_sentence: If you cannot attend, be sure to inform me in advance.

learning_objective: Can use 'be sure to', 'be certain to' to talk about certainty.
example_sentence: A person that will act in that way is sure to be successful.

learning_objective: Can use 'be forced to' to talk about an unnamed obligation, where the circumstances are beyond the control of the speaker/writer.
example_sentence: I don't like to be forced to do something that I don't want to do.

learning_objective: Can use 'be obliged to' to talk about an external obligation, especially in institutional contexts.
example_sentence: The Sales Representatives are obliged to participate.

learning_objective: Can use 'you (have) got to' to make a strong suggestion.
example_sentence: If you think you have seen almost everything in this world, you've got to see this museum.

learning_objective: Can use 'may' with an increasing range of adverbs (most commonly 'even', 'only', 'already', 'never', 'just', 'sometimes') in the normal mid-position after the modal verb. ► adverbs
example_sentence: When you're reading books, you may even find words you don't know and if you're an interested person, you will try to find out what it means, thus improving your vocabulary.

learning_objective: Can use 'may I' to make a polite request, command or suggestion in formal contexts.
example_sentence: May I have your attention please?

learning_objective: Can use 'may have' + '-ed' to talk about possibility in the past.
example_sentence: I think that he may have forgiven me for that accident … !

learning_objective: Can use 'may I' to make a polite request for permission.
example_sentence: Sir, may I come in?

learning_objective: Can use 'may' in phrases such as as 'you may know', or 'as you may have' + '-ed' to focus the reader on shared knowledge.
example_sentence: I usually take more than one book because, as you may know, it takes many hours to get there.

learning_objective: Can use 'may … but' to express an unexpected point of view.
example_sentence: This may surprise you but I've managed to lose some weight, which I'm proud of.

learning_objective: Can use the question form.
example_sentence: Is it possible to use the prize next year or must I cancel my trip to India?

learning_objective: Can use 'must have' + '-ed'.
example_sentence: When I realized that he must have forgotten, I called him up.

learning_objective: Can use the fixed expression 'I must say' to give emphasis, usually about something positive.
example_sentence: I must say that I'm most delighted to have you here with us, Mr. Saramago, and it's with enormous pleasure that my students will hear your words.

learning_objective: Can use 'must' with an increasing range of adverbs (including 'all', 'never', 'strongly', 'first') in the normal mid position after the modal verb. ► adverbs
example_sentence: To sum up, we must all act immediately because the existence of humanity may be in danger.

learning_objective: Can use 'must' to ask about obligation and necessity.
example_sentence: Is it possible to use the prize next year or must I cancel my trip to India?

learning_objective: Can use the perfect form of 'must' to make deductions about the past.
example_sentence: Of course, India must have changed a lot since Jules Verne wrote his book, but I think that its charm remains.

learning_objective: Can use the fixed expression 'I must admit' or 'you must admit' to express concession.
example_sentence: On the other hand you must admit that not all ready-made meals taste really good and often they are not as healthy as fresh ones.

learning_objective: Can use the negative forms of 'must' to talk about what is not permitted.
example_sentence: You must not hit them or punish them without good reason if you do not want them to become your worst enemy.

learning_objective: Can use 'needn't to' talk about a lack of obligation.
example_sentence: I like summer so much because the weather is hot and we needn't wear a jacket.

learning_objective: Can use the negative form.
example_sentence: You needn't worry about where we can stay.

learning_objective: Can use 'ought to' without a following verb where the previous main verb is understood. ► ellipsis
example_sentence: Otherwise some students would not get as much specialised help as they ought to.

learning_objective: Can use 'ought to' to talk about desired states of affairs.
example_sentence: Furthermore, we ought to take care of our health by going on trips to not unpolluted places as often as possible.

learning_objective: Can use 'should' and 'shouldn't' without a following verb where the previous main verb is understood.
example_sentence: Well, have you ever checked your blood pressure? I think you should.

learning_objective: Can use 'should be' + '-ing'.
example_sentence: Well, you should be bouncing up and down with joy (just kidding)!

learning_objective: Can use 'should be' + '-ing' to talk about present general obligation.
example_sentence: Wild animals should be running free but I think that zoos today have an important role in keeping them alive.

learning_objective: Can use 'should be' + '-ing' to talk about expectations.
Can use 'used to' without a following verb where the previous main verb is understood. ► ellipsis
example_sentence: She doesn't travel as much as she used to.

learning_objective: Can use 'will' (particularly 'Will you please') to make requests and commands in a wide range of contexts.
example_sentence: Will you please send me his name, address and telephone number.

learning_objective: Can use 'would' to talk about habitual actions and events in the past.
example_sentence: [talking about a job that the speaker did last year] In my opinion I worked very hard. I would spend a lot of hours at work.

learning_objective: Can use uncontracted 'not' for emphasis or in formal contexts.
example_sentence: Do not take on too many assignments at a time.

learning_objective: Can use negative forms 'neither of' and 'none of' + pronoun or noun phrase with the affirmative form of the verb.
example_sentence: Neither of us is liable in this case.

learning_objective: Can use 'neither … nor' to connect two words, phrases or clauses, often to give emphasis or focus.
example_sentence: Neither you nor I would like that.

learning_objective: Can use 'never' in front position followed by an inverted subject (most commonly 'I') + main verb, to give focus.
example_sentence: Never have I seen such a realistic and impressive film before.

learning_objective: Can form noun phrases with plural nouns + ' + noun.
example_sentence: He had promised that he would go to his grandparents' house this afternoon.

learning_objective: Can form noun phrases with noun + 'of' + possessive determiner + noun + '‘s', particularly with 'friend' or 'friends'.
example_sentence: Actually, I know a great one and they can make a little discount for us because the owner is a friend of my father's.

learning_objective: Can form complex noun phrases by using adjective phrases as postmodifiers to give more information about the noun.
example_sentence: Besides, we could leave one day in the morning and go by boat to a small and exotic island full of different and exotic species of birds that we could observe and photograph.

guideword: FORM: COMPLEX NOUN PHRASES WITH ADJECTIVES COMBINED WITH 'BUT'
learning_objective: Can form an increasing range of complex noun phrases with more than one adjective combined with 'but'.
example_sentence: I am very lucky, because I was born in a small but beautiful city called Neuva Helvecia.

learning_objective: Can use the '-ing' form of verbs as nouns in subject position.
example_sentence: Working until midnight every evening is not reasonable. Working late is not the solution and it is even dangerous.

learning_objective: Can use uncountable nouns without a definite article to refer to an abstract thing in general.
example_sentence: Television is more attractive because it has moving images.

learning_objective: Can use have + object + -ed to talk about something where the speaker is in a pro-active or a passive role.
example_sentence: After I left you on Monday, I had my car serviced and visited Washington with its wonderful memorials.

learning_objective: Can use a reflexive pronoun with the 'get'-passive.
example_sentence: Although to drive a car you must be very careful because you could get yourself killed if you don't drive carefully enough.

learning_objective: Can use 'get' + object + 'to'-infinitive to talk about causing someone to do something.
example_sentence: I would like to point at a few things about our small town, which I, as a citizen, have to explain to our friends, in order to get them to visit us more frequently.

learning_objective: Can use the passive infinitive affirmative and negative forms after an increasing range of main verbs, modal verbs, adjectives and nouns, in impersonal constructions.
example_sentence: According to your advertisement, some training is supposed to be given.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect passive affirmative form.
example_sentence: The car had been serviced and everything seemed to be all right.

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect simple affirmative and negative forms with modal verbs to refer to the past.
example_sentence: I think that all these problems could have been avoided and that asking for my money back is the best way of forgetting that evening.

learning_objective: Can use the past continuous passive affirmative.
example_sentence: However, to my disappointment, the restaurant was closed because it was being redecorated.

learning_objective: Can use past simple passive negative.
example_sentence: What is worse, the ticket price was not reduced despite the fact that I showed my student ID.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect passive negative form.
example_sentence: [talking about a car] In fact, it hadn't been stolen, it had been taken by a man and his wife, because their car broke down and because she was about to have her baby …

learning_objective: Can use the present continuous passive affirmative with an increasing range of verbs.
example_sentence: This year's topic is the online training for employees, which is being relaunched.

learning_objective: Can use the present continuous passive negative.
example_sentence: … students are not being educated equally.

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect passive affirmative form (often in the context of reporting).  ► reported speech
example_sentence: I have been asked to write a report about an accident which happened to me last Saturday.

learning_objective: Can use the passive with modal verbs to evaluate or summarise.
example_sentence: In conclusion, it can be seen that the bicycle is more suitable for those who are living in a little town or in the country.

guideword: FORM: WITH VERBS TAKING TWO OBJECTS, , WITH PREPOSITIONAL PHRASE
learning_objective: Can use the passive with a range of tenses and verbs needing two objects (e.g. give, offer, sell) with the direct object in subject position and the indirect object in a prepositional phrase.
example_sentence: Your name was given to me by a member of yours, Allan Westwood, whom I met last week.

learning_objective: Can use the passive with 'will' to talk about the future.
example_sentence: Just to let you know you will be booked into the Palace Hotel …

learning_objective: Can use the passive with modal verbs in a range of contexts, with a variety of subjects.
example_sentence: Architects should be hired to design parks, where people could go for a walk or have a picnic.

guideword: FORM: WITH VERBS TAKING TWO OBJECTS.
learning_objective: Can use the passive with a wide range of verbs needing two objects, putting the indirect object in subject position.
example_sentence: I was very happy to hear that you have been offered two jobs, one in a restaurant and one in a museum.

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect passive negative form (often in the context of reporting). ► reported speech
example_sentence: …the old-fashioned house which has not been used for twenty years is not a very attractive sight from our town either.

learning_objective: Can use the past continuous with a wide range of adverbs in the normal mid position.
example_sentence: My younger brother was constantly bothering me.

learning_objective: Can use the past continuous to make a request or suggestions more polite or less direct.
example_sentence: I was wondering if I could impose on you again for my next visit.

learning_objective: Can use the negative form.
example_sentence: Laura realised that she had not been studying very hard lately.

guideword: FORM/USE: BACKGROUND INFORMATION WITH TIME CONJUNCTION
learning_objective: Can use the past perfect continuous after a time conjunction to give background information.
example_sentence: After we had been sunbathing at the beach all day long, we went every evening to a cosy restaurant called "The Barking Cat", where we had a good meal and some wine.

guideword: FORM/USE: BACKGROUND INFORMATION WITH RELATIVE CLAUSE
learning_objective: Can use the past perfect continuous in a relative clause to give background information.  ► relative clauses
example_sentence: And there were two men who had been chasing the murderer to find out who the man is and rescue the girl....

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect continuous to talk about events which started before a time in the past and which finished, but where the effects or results were still important at a point in the past.
example_sentence: Unfortunately it had been snowing for a long time and so I was unable to go out for a walk.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect continuous with adverbs in the normal mid position.
example_sentence: I had been working hard at my job and I had really been looking forward to this holiday.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect simple after 'because' to give explanatory information.
example_sentence: He was sleeping but I realised that this was because he had lost a lot of blood.

guideword: FORM/USE: AFTER 'IF ONLY' AND 'WISH', IMAGINED PAST
learning_objective: Can use the affirmative form after 'if only', and the affirmative and negative forms after 'wish' to talk about regret.
example_sentence: If only I had listened to my father!

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect simple with a wide range of adverbs (including 'finally', 'recently', 'simply') in the normal mid-position.  ► adverbs
example_sentence: He had finally achieved his goal!

learning_objective: Can invert the subject and auxiliary verb with 'no sooner … than' to talk about something that happened immediately before something else, often in formal contexts.
example_sentence: … but no sooner had I gone to bed than someone broke the door down.

learning_objective: Can use question forms.
example_sentence: Had I made the same mistake twice?

learning_objective: Can invert the subject and auxiliary verb after 'never (before)' to talk about a unique event, often in formal contexts.
example_sentence: Never had I been in such a ridiculous situation!

learning_objective: Can leave out the subject before the past perfect simple where it is understood from the previous clause.
example_sentence: But now she felt stronger and had lost her fear.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect simple to report statements, where the main verb in the reporting clause is in the past simple.  ► reported speech
example_sentence: […] one morning she was listening to the radio, when suddenly the announcer said that the police had arrested a man who had stolen five expensive and unique paintings.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect simple to talk about situations which changed.
example_sentence: After the show we had planned to go to the theatre restaurant. But it was closed.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect simple in relative clauses to give background information.    ► relative clauses
example_sentence: After a while Mr and Mrs Landsbury, the couple who had organised the party, got everybody's attention and […] asked everybody to stay calm.

learning_objective: Can use the past perfect simple to report questions, where the main verb in the reporting clause is in the past simple.  ► reported speech
example_sentence: I asked him who had given him my phone number and he said it had been me.

learning_objective: Can use 'yes/no', 'wh-', tag and negative question forms with an increasing range of verbs.
example_sentence: Are you still looking for a job or did you succeed in finding one?

learning_objective: Can use the negative form with a wide range of regular and irregular verbs.
example_sentence: I didn't hesitate and went there straight away.

learning_objective: Can use the past simple with a range of subordinating conjunctions, including 'as soon as', 'before', 'if', 'once', 'since', 'so', 'until', 'when', 'while'.
example_sentence: … as soon as we went down to around 15 metres in depth, we saw a lot of coloured fish.

learning_objective: Can use a range of time adjuncts with the past simple.
example_sentence: I also worked as a helper on a summer camp for primary schoolchildren in Canada last summer.

learning_objective: Can use the past simple with 'I wondered' and 'I wanted' as politeness structures, when making polite requests and thanking.
example_sentence: So, I wondered if you could introduce me to somebody who knows how to use a camera, so that I will be able to record my trip for you.

learning_objective: Can use the past simple after 'if' as a politeness structure, especially in letters and emails.
example_sentence: I think it would be very helpful if I told you my own case.

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect continuous with adverbs in the normal mid position.
example_sentence: I have just been reading an article in your newspaper this morning, about The Westfield Music Festival.

learning_objective: Can use the negative form.
example_sentence: I hope that I have not been wasting your time and giving you too much trouble.

learning_objective: Can use the present perfect continuous to focus on a finished activity in the recent past but where the effects or results are still important or relevant.
example_sentence: They might be slippery when it has been snowing.

learning_objective: Can use the question form with an increasing range of verbs.
example_sentence: How has your family been?

learning_objective: Can use the negative form of the present perfect simple with 'still' to emphasise that something that was expected to happen continues not to happen.  ► adverbs
example_sentence: I still haven't read the role but when I read it, I was very excited and I accepted Mr Brown's offer.

learning_objective: Can use less frequent simple prepositions, often in a more formal, literary or business context.
example_sentence: Suddenly the lights went out and a kind of silent screaming was heard throughout the whole dark room.

learning_objective: Can use preposition + relative pronoun as complement, to avoid preposition stranding, often in formal contexts. ► Clauses: relative
example_sentence: According to your advertisement in a language magazine, you have a vacancy in which I am very interested.

learning_objective: Can use a wide range of complex prepositions.
example_sentence: She did not say very much except for "oh" and "yes, yes" every once in a while.

guideword: FORM/USE: WITH ADVERBS OF INDEFINITE FREQUENCY
learning_objective: Can use the present continuous with an increasing range of adverbs of indefinite frequency, often to talk about surprising or undesirable situations or events.
example_sentence: … scientists don't really get together with top models … they are constantly studying and reading books, whereas the job of a top model is actually based on physical appearance.

guideword: FORM: INVERSION WITH 'NOT ONLY … BUT ALSO'
learning_objective: Can use the inverted form of the past simple with auxiliary 'do', in the phrase 'not only … but also'.
example_sentence: Not only do they save you filing time, but also they save office space.

learning_objective: Can use the present simple to summarise events or plot in a story or piece of history, often to give immediate dramatic interest.
example_sentence: The plot of the book takes place in Vienna after the Second World War.

learning_objective: Can use the present simple with a wide range of speech act verbs, including '(dis)agree', 'accept', 'advise'.
example_sentence: We disagree with the new numbering system as it is too complicated.

learning_objective: Can use 'those' as a substitute, followed by a relative clause or '-ed' or '-ing'.
example_sentence: Moreover, for those who are not interested in sports and who prefer shopping, it would be a good idea to prevent cars and especially lorries from running in the center of the town.

learning_objective: Can use 'these ones', 'those ones' as a substitute for plural countable nouns that have previously been mentioned.
example_sentence: This is only the beginning; there are a lot of things that should be done, but let's just start with these ones.

learning_objective: Can use 'we' and 'us' to refer to people in general.
example_sentence: These days the car is everywhere, we can see cars all over the world.

learning_objective: Can use 'they/them' to refer back to indefinite pronouns when we do not know the number or gender.
example_sentence: If someone is watching a film that they don't like they can always choose another channel to watch to.

learning_objective: Can use 'he/she', 'he' or 'she' or 'they' to refer back to gender neutral singular nouns or indefinite pronouns when we are not sure of the gender.
example_sentence: If a child had free time, he/she would play on his/her own and not with other children.

learning_objective: Can use indefinite pronouns with a relative clause to form complex noun phrases, to give focus.
example_sentence: Something that I like very much is the idea of the classes.

learning_objective: Can use the full range of indefinite pronouns as subjects, with a singular verb.
example_sentence: But, nowadays nothing is free, so you have to pay for everything.

learning_objective: Can use indefinite pronouns in vague expressions to refer to things in a non-specific way.
example_sentence: Is it worth having a nervous breakdown or anything like that?

learning_objective: Can use the possessive pronoun 'ours' with singular and plural reference, in object positions, and complement positions after 'be' and after prepositions.
example_sentence: Can we rent windsurf boards or should we bring ours with us?

learning_objective: Can use the possessive pronouns 'hers' and 'ours' in comparative clauses.
example_sentence: I told my mother that your Spanish omelette is better than hers.

learning_objective: Can use the possessive pronoun 'ours' after noun + 'of'.
example_sentence: Finally we went to Venice where we met some friends of ours and spent with them the rest of the holiday.

learning_objective: Can use the possessive pronoun 'ours', with singular and plural reference, in subject position.
example_sentence: Ours is the only store in this locality which opens at 7.00 am. (Business English Certificate (Vantage); B2; Tamil)

learning_objective: Can use the possessive pronoun 'hers', with singular reference, in object positions, and complement positions after 'be' and after prepositions.
example_sentence: At the entrance, the security guard asked for my identity card. Pat gave hers.

learning_objective: Can use the possessive pronoun 'theirs', with singular and plural reference, in object positions, and complement positions after 'be' and after prepositions.
example_sentence: I could show them the traditional things of our country and learn theirs.

learning_objective: Can use pronouns 'each', 'either', 'enough', 'neither', 'several' as subject and object pronouns.
example_sentence: As you could guess, each has a very unique style.

learning_objective: Can use a wide range of pronouns ('neither', 'either', 'none') with 'of' followed by an object pronoun.
example_sentence: Stella and her boyfriend,... lived with Chuck at the same house, but neither of them knew that Chuck had passed a very complicated exam to be a spy for the United States.

learning_objective: Can use 'one another' as the object of a verb or complement of a preposition to talk about the mutual behaviour of two or more people, often in formal contexts.
example_sentence: I also thought to create a studying space where students, staff and visitors can meet one another.

learning_objective: Can use plural reflexive pronouns with 'by' to mean alone, without any help.
example_sentence: A friend of mine and I are planning to spend a short holiday by ourselves.

learning_objective: Can use the plural reflexive pronoun 'yourselves' for politeness.
example_sentence: Dear Sir/Madam, I am writing to you in response to the advertisement published by yourselves in "the Traveller" regarding The Trip of a Lifetime.

learning_objective: Can use the singular pronoun 'itself' for emphasis.
example_sentence: In my opinion, the main characters do not have to be liked by readers but the story itself must be good and interesting to be successful.

learning_objective: Can use plural reflexive pronouns after prepositions where the object of the preposition is the same as the subject of the verb.
example_sentence: Doing sports is an excellent way to relax and it can help us to feel good about ourselves.

guideword: FORM: SAME SUBJECT AND OBJECT, PLURAL
learning_objective: Can use plural reflexive pronouns to refer to actions where the subject and object of the verb are the same.
example_sentence: In this way we will be able to enjoy ourselves even if this is not well-paid work.

learning_objective: Can use plural reflexive pronouns for emphasis.
example_sentence: The food is not very good, but sometimes we can prepare something to eat ourselves.

learning_objective: Can use the reflexive pronoun 'itself' with 'by' to mean alone or without any help.
example_sentence: If an animal is kept by itself , with no one to keep it company, it will get lonely and very sad.

learning_objective: Can use the fixed expression 'in itself' as an intensifier .
example_sentence: I would also spend much more quality time with my family as a whole, which is quite important in itself.

learning_objective: Can use 'it' as an object with 'make' to introduce something the speaker or writer is going to refer to.
example_sentence: This shyness makes it hard for me to speak in public, or even to go out with my friends as often as I should.

learning_objective: Can leave out the subject pronoun with a limited range of verbs, in informal contexts. ► ellipsis
example_sentence: Must rush now because I have to go out with Paul.

learning_objective: Can use 'one' as a generic personal pronoun in the subject position to mean people in general. ► generic pronouns
example_sentence: If one doesn't live close enough to walk to work, one can choose to get there by bicycle or by car.

learning_objective: Can use dummy 'it' with 'appears', 'feels', 'looks' and 'seems'.
example_sentence: When you watch a film, it seems to be that you are inside it, and you are a character in it.

learning_objective: Can use 'these ones', 'those ones' as a substitute for plural countable nouns which have already been mentioned or are obvious from the context.
example_sentence: This is only the beginning; there are a lot of things that should be done, but let's just start with these ones.

guideword: FORM/USE: TAGS WITH IMPERATIVES AS SOFTENERS
learning_objective: Can use an affirmative or negative tag after an imperative clause to soften the imperative.
example_sentence: Think about it, will you?

guideword: FORM: NEGATIVE QUESTIONS WITH MAIN VERBS
learning_objective: Can use 'wh-'words + the negative form of auxiliary 'do' + subject + main verb to form 'wh-'questions.
example_sentence: You still have this agonising headache. Why don't you go to your doctor?

learning_objective: Can modify the reporting verb with an adverb.
example_sentence: "So this is a real cow, mom?" she asked doubtfully.

guideword: FORM: DIRECT SPEECH, REPORTING CLAUSE, MID POSITION
learning_objective: Can report speech and thought directly using the reporting verb in the mid position of the reported clause.
example_sentence: "No problem", I thought, "there is only one person".

guideword: FORM: DIRECT SPEECH, REPORTING CLAUSE, INVERTED SUBJECT
learning_objective: Can report speech directly inverting the subject and verb in the reporting clause where the subject is a proper noun or noun phrase.
example_sentence: "I was astonished when I saw them standing there", said John.

learning_objective: Can use the past continuous of the reporting verb to report mental processes, with a finite or non-finite 'wh-'clause in the reported clause.
example_sentence: I was wondering why I failed.

learning_objective: Can use the present simple form of the reporting verb to report information from a written source and a present tense verb in the reported clause.
example_sentence: First of all, your advertisement says that you have got two great nights in a hotel for us.

guideword: FORM: REPORTED REQUESTS AND COMMANDS, NEGATIVE
learning_objective: Can report requests and commands with 'ask' or 'tell' + direct object + 'not' + 'to-'infinitive.
example_sentence: Meanwhile, she told me not to be scared but the place was very... old.

learning_objective: Can report speech about events in the past with backshifts for tenses and words relating to time.
example_sentence: She had told me the previous day that she had something important to tell me.

learning_objective: Can use a wide range of linking verbs with complements.
example_sentence: There are indeed many ways of doing this and it all seems very confusing.

learning_objective: Can introduce a new subject before the '-ing' form (using noun or object pronoun).
Can follow some verbs with a 'to'-infinitive or an '-ing' form, with a change in meaning.
example_sentence: He said if you need to change the time, remember to contact him to make a new appointment.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of verbs followed by an '-ing' form.
example_sentence: [talking about cars]... we can't avoid using them because we are used to them.

learning_objective: Can use some verbs connected with the senses + direct object + an '-ing' form, to give emphasis to an ongoing activity.
example_sentence: Then I heard someone screaming, I was afraid and I said to the others: "Let's go out, something is happening".

learning_objective: Can use a wide range of phrasal verbs without an object.
example_sentence: Some species are dying out, and keeping such animals in zoos where they can give life to new generations is the only way to prevent them from becoming completely extinct.

learning_objective: Can use phrasal verbs + nouns as object + particle.
example_sentence: I am glad that you have decided to take some time off and more glad because you are going to the U.S.

learning_objective: Can use a wide range of phrasal verbs + particle + object.
example_sentence: I don't need to use my imagination while I'm surfing the internet, but if I'm reading a book I can build up the whole surroundings and the characters in my own head.

learning_objective: Can use an increasing range of verb + particle + preposition + noun or pronoun.
example_sentence: I can look up to someone in admiration if he (or she) is dedicating his (or her) life to helping other people.

learning_objective: Can use an adverb between the verb and the preposition.
example_sentence: I am really willing to know more about horses and to learn how to deal properly with them.

learning_objective: Can use verb + preposition, where the preposition is separated from its complement.
example_sentence: In Poland rules that refer to how to dress at school aren't very strict; we don't wear any uniforms, but our clothes have to be clean and nice to look at.

learning_objective: Can use 'there' with other verbs with modal meaning + 'be' + complement.
example_sentence: I figured out that there had to be matches somewhere; Bob, who owned the cabin, had told me that it was well equipped.

learning_objective: Can use semi-modal auxiliary verbs, 'dare' and 'need'.
example_sentence: You needn't worry about where we can stay.
